Clarke makes the universal application: every Jew is necessarily under the curse of God's broken law, and every sinner under the same. Barnes notes that the proposition is general and the argument is designed to show that from the nature of the case justification by works of the law is impossible — under all circumstances of obedience, a person is still left with the law's heavy curse resting upon them, since the standard is perfect and perpetual compliance. Wesley makes the final point economically: the law requires what no man can perform — perfect, uninterrupted, and perpetual obedience.

voices.html: The argument is drawn from the contradictory nature of the two schemes; for the same fountain does not yield both hot and cold. The law holds all living men under its curse; and from the law, therefore, it is in vain to expect a blessing. They are declared to be cursed who place their trust for salvation in those works, for the controversy here relates to the method of obtaining the righteousness of faith.

In this and the following verses the action of the Law is contrasted with that of faith. The antithesis is: faith — blessing; law — curse. The curse was the penalty which the Law itself imposed upon all who failed to keep it. None really kept it, and therefore none escaped this curse. As many as are of the works of the law — an expression corresponding to "they which be of faith" in verse 9.

As many as are of the works of the law — all that seek salvation by the performance of the works of the law are under the curse, because it is impossible for them to come up to the spiritual meaning and intent of the law; and the law pronounces them cursed that continue not in all things written in the book of the law to do them. Hence, every Jew is necessarily under the curse of God's broken law; and every sinner under the same.

They only receive it. For as many as are of the works of the law — as God deals with on that footing, only on the terms the law proposes, are under a curse; for it is written, Cursed is every one who continueth not in all the things which are written in the law. Who continueth not in all the things — so it requires what no man can perform, namely, perfect, uninterrupted, and perpetual obedience. Deu 27:26.

11.synthesis: No man is justified before God by the law, and the proof is Habakkuk 2:4: "the righteous shall live by faith." Calvin frames this as a second argument from contradictory schemes: if we are justified by faith, it is not by law; but we are justified by faith; therefore it is not by law. Ellicott focuses on the word "faith" — the stress falls here, just as in verse 12 the stress will fall on "doeth." The just man is just not before the exercise of faith but by it, and his life — his standing before God — is maintained by the continuance of faith. Wesley's summary is the most precise: the man who is accounted just before God shall continue in a state of acceptance, life, and salvation by faith — this is the way God has chosen, not the way of obedience to an impossibly demanding standard. Clarke notes that Habakkuk declared this under the direct influence of the Spirit of God, which gives it particular authority in an argument that appeals to Scripture. Barnes identifies the positive side of the logic: God has appointed faith as the condition on which men shall live, and having done so, he has precluded the possibility of obtaining salvation in any other mode — there cannot be two ways of obtaining life simultaneously operative.

voices.html: He again argues from a comparison of contradictory schemes. "If we are justified by faith, it is not by the law: but we are justified by faith; therefore it is not by the law." The minor is proved by a passage from Habakkuk. The major is proved by the difference in the methods of justification: the law justifies him who fulfils all its precepts, while faith justifies him who embraces Christ as the fulfiller.

In the sight of God — standing as a prisoner before his tribunal. The just shall live by faith — the stress is on the word "faith." It is faith (not law) which gives life. In Paul's application, the word "just" must be taken in a slightly proleptic sense — a man is not just before the exercise of faith, but he becomes just by the exercise of it; and in another aspect, the just man lives by the continuance of faith.

But that no man is justified by the law — by the observance of the law — is evident; for the Prophet Habakkuk, Hab 2:4, has declared under the direct influence of the Spirit of God: The just shall live by faith; or, he who is just by faith shall live. Therefore this justification comes not by works or the observance of the law, but by faith.

But that none is justified by his obedience to the law in the sight of God — whatever may be done in the sight of man — is farther evident from the words of Habakkuk: The just shall live by faith — that is, the man who is accounted just before God shall continue in a state of acceptance, life, and salvation, by faith. This is the way God hath chosen. Hab 2:4.

12.synthesis: The law is not of faith: it operates on a different principle entirely — doing, not believing. Paul quotes Leviticus 18:5: "the one who does them shall live by them." Calvin is careful to insist that the law is not contrary to faith in an absolute sense — God is not divided against himself — but the contradiction between law and faith lies specifically in the matter of justification: fire and water are easier to unite than the propositions "men are justified by faith" and "men are justified by the law." Ellicott draws the contrast with verse 11: there the stress was on "faith"; here it falls on "doeth" — the law deals in doing, not believing, and the two systems are mutually exclusive as paths to righteousness. Clarke is the most economical: the law does not say "What do you believe?" but "What have you done?" Wesley agrees: the law is "quite opposite" to faith — it does not say "Believe" but "Do." Barnes explains that the law does not make provision for faith but requires unwavering and perpetual obedience — a standard that, as verse 10 established, no one meets.

voices.html: The law evidently is not contrary to faith; otherwise God would be unlike himself. But the contradiction between the law and faith lies in the matter of justification. You will more easily unite fire and water than reconcile these two statements: that men are justified by faith, and that they are justified by the law. The law is not of faith because it demands doing, not believing.

The law is not of faith — the ruling principle of the Law is not faith, but works. The man that doeth them — just as the stress was upon "faith" in the last verse, so here it falls on the word "doeth." It is a matter of works. The two systems are mutually exclusive as paths to the one righteousness God requires.

And the law is not of faith — it promises no forgiveness to believing, but requires obedience. It is not, What do you believe? but, What have you done? The man that doeth them perfectly, at all times, and in all places, he shall live in them; but if in any case he fails, he forfeits his life.
And the law is not of faith — but quite opposite to it: it does not say, Believe; but, Do. Lev 18:5.

13.synthesis: The pivot of the chapter: "Christ redeemed us from the curse of the law by becoming a curse for us." Ellicott opens with a translation note — "Christ redeemed us" rather than "hath redeemed us" — and observes that the opening without a connecting particle gives the verse sharpness and emphasis: the law brought a curse; that was all it could do; the first thing Christianity does is undo this by deliverance from the curse. The deliverance is represented as a ransom — Christ bought the human race off from the penalty of the law. Calvin explains the mechanism: the apostle had made all under the law subject to the curse, and having stated this difficulty he meets it by showing that Christ has made us free, which further advances his purpose — if we are saved by being freed from the curse, then righteousness is not by the law. Clarke gives the most detailed treatment of the Deuteronomy 21:23 background: the body of an executed criminal was hung on a tree as a sign that God's curse had fallen on the person, and Christ willingly submitted to this form of death to take that curse upon himself. Wesley's compressed statement captures the logic: Christ took the curse upon himself, submitting to that death which the law pronounces peculiarly accursed, that we might be delivered from it. Barnes distinguishes the Greek word used here (exegorasen) — more general than lutroo, which strictly means ransom — but notes the idea of purchase is primary: Christ bought us out from under the curse at the price of his own life.

voices.html: The apostle had made all who are under the law subject to the curse; from which arose this great difficulty, that the Jews could not free themselves. Having stated this difficulty, he meets it by showing that Christ hath made us free. If we are saved because we have been freed from the curse of the law, then righteousness is not by the law. He next points out the manner: by bearing the curse himself.

Christ redeemed us — better, Christ redeemed us. The opening of this verse without any connecting particle lends sharpness and emphasis to the contrast. The Law brought a curse. There it stopped short. That was all it could do. The first thing that Christianity does is to undo this result of the Law by deliverance from the curse — a deliverance represented as a ransom.

Christ hath redeemed us — Exegorasen — hath bought us with a price, viz. his blood, or life. Being made a curse for us — being made an atonement for our sins; for whatever was offered as an atonement was considered as bearing the punishment due to sin. In the same day in which a criminal was executed it was ordered that his body should not remain overnight upon the tree — Christ willingly submitted to this death to bear the curse in our place. Deu 21:23.

Christ — Christ alone. The abruptness of the sentence shows a holy indignation at those who reject so great a blessing. Hath redeemed us — whether Jews or Gentiles, at a high price. From the curse of the law — the curse of God, which the law denounces against all transgressors of it. Being made a curse for us — taking the curse upon himself, that we might be delivered from it, willingly submitting to that death which the law pronounces peculiarly accursed. Deu 21:23.

14.synthesis: The purpose of Christ's redemption from the curse is stated in two parallel clauses: that the blessing of Abraham might come on the Gentiles through Jesus Christ, and that we — both Jews and Gentiles — might receive the promised Spirit through faith. Calvin connects this directly to the previous verse: if the Jews must be delivered from the law in order to become heirs of Abraham, what shall hinder the Gentiles from obtaining the same benefit? And if that blessing is found in Christ alone, it is faith in Christ that alone brings it to all. Ellicott reads the verse as the hinge that connects the cross to the Pentecost promise — the abolition of the law on the cross opened the Messianic inheritance equally to Gentiles, and the outpouring of the Spirit accompanied this opening. Clarke identifies what "the blessing of Abraham" means concretely: justification, the pardon of sin, peace with God, spiritual life, and eternal glory — all the blessings consequent on being Abraham's heir. Wesley brings the two clauses together economically: the blessing promised to Abraham comes to the Gentiles also, and we — whether Jews or Gentiles — receive the promise of the Spirit through faith, not works. Barnes emphasizes the equal scope: since Christ has been made a curse for all without exclusive reference to Jews or any other nation, the blessing flows equally to all who believe.

voices.html: Having said that Christ hath redeemed us from the curse of the law, he now applies that statement more closely to his purpose. The promised blessing of Abraham is founded on this and flows from it to the Gentiles. If the Jews must be delivered from the law in order to become the heirs of Abraham, what shall hinder the Gentiles from obtaining the same benefit? And if that blessing is found in Christ alone, it is faith in Christ that alone brings it.

The abolition of the Law, consummated upon the cross, involved the doing away of all the old restrictions which confined the Messianic inheritance to the Jews. Henceforth this inheritance, and the promised outpouring of the Spirit which was to accompany it, was open equally to the Gentiles. The one condition now was faith, and that intimate relation to the Messiah which faith implied.

That the blessing of Abraham — that is, justification or the pardon of sin, with all other blessings consequent on it, such as peace with God, spiritual life, and eternal glory. Might come on the Gentiles through Jesus Christ — he was made a curse for us, that the blessings promised to Abraham might be given to them who believe on him as having been made a curse, an expiatory victim, for them.

That the blessing of Abraham — the blessing promised to him. Might come on the gentiles — also. That we — who believe, whether Jews or gentiles. Might receive the promise of the Spirit — which includes all the other promises. Through faith — not by works; for faith looks wholly to the promise.

15.synthesis: Paul now shifts from biblical argument to a civil analogy: even in human affairs, once a covenant is ratified no one annuls or adds to it. The point is an a fortiori: if men honor sealed agreements, how much more must God's covenant with Abraham be honored and kept unmodified? Ellicott notes the phrase "I speak after the manner of men" signals that the figure is drawn from ordinary civil relations — and that a fortiori argument in things relating to God is thereby heightened, since men may change and break solemn engagements while God is absolutely faithful and unchangeable. Calvin uses this to put the Galatians to shame: it is highly disgraceful that the testimony of God should have less weight with believers than that of a mortal man; in demanding that God's sacred covenant receive at least the deference given to ordinary human transactions, he is not placing God on a level with men but invoking a minimum standard. Wesley makes the precise point: even a human covenant, once legally confirmed, cannot be altered by the covenanter himself unless something unforeseen occurs — and nothing is unforeseen with God. Barnes explains the illustration: when a contract involving obligations and promises is made, no one can add to it or take from it; so with God's promise to Abraham.

voices.html: By this expression he intended to put them to the blush. It is highly disgraceful and base that the testimony of God should have less weight with us than that of a mortal man. In demanding that the sacred covenant of God shall receive not less deference than is commonly yielded to ordinary human transactions, he does not place God on a level with men — the immense distance between God and men is still left for their consideration.

The figure is taken from the ordinary civil relations between man and man, and therefore supplies an à fortiori argument in things relating to God — for men may change and break the most solemn engagements; God is absolutely faithful and unchangeable. The phrase "I speak after the manner of men" is found in similar form in Romans 3:5; 6:19; 1 Corinthians 9:8.

I speak after the manner of men — I illustrate this by a familiar instance, taken from the practice of men. Though it be but a man's covenant, yet, if it be once legally confirmed, none — no, not the covenanter himself, unless something unforeseen occur, which cannot be the case with God — disannulleth, or addeth thereto any new conditions.

I draw an illustration from what actually occurs among men. When a contract or agreement is made by men involving obligations and promises, no one can add to it or take from it. So with God. He made a solemn promise to Abraham. That promise pertained to his posterity. The blessing was connected with that promise, and it was of the nature of a compact with Abraham — no later addition can affect it.

16.synthesis: The promises were made to Abraham and his seed — singular, not plural. Paul argues that the singular "seed" points to Christ as the one in whom the promise reaches its fulfillment. Ellicott explains this as a parenthetical observation: the point the Judaisers could not contest was that the promise pointed to the Messiah; Paul's argument shows that it therefore reaches fruition in the Messianic age, not in the ethnic descendants of Abraham generally. Calvin, while affirming the theological argument, offers a caution: the Hebrew word for seed can mean either singular or plural, and Paul is not making a purely linguistic argument — the main point is that Christ is the "blessed seed" through whom all nations were to be blessed, and the singular is meaningful theologically even if the grammatical proof could be questioned. Clarke notes the practical meaning: the promise was made to Abraham and his one particular kind of posterity — those who believe, who are children of Abraham because they are believers. Wesley identifies "Christ" as including all who believe in him — the seed is Christ as head together with the body of believers. Barnes gives the fullest treatment of the grammatical question and concurs that the weight of the argument is theological: the promise is fulfilled in the Messiah and in those who are in him.

voices.html: Before pursuing his argument, he introduces an observation about the substance of the covenant, that it rests on Christ alone. But if Christ be the foundation, it follows that it is of free grace. To prove that God speaks of Christ, he calls attention to the singular number — but we must not strain this as if it were a purely grammatical argument; the point is theological: Christ is the one blessed seed through whom all nations receive blessing.

A parenthetical explanation of the true object of the promise. That promise was shown by its wording to have reference to the Messiah. It did not speak of "seeds," but of "seed" — not of "descendants" but of "descendant." The object of this parenthesis is to prove a point the Judaisers would not contest: the fulfillment of the promise to Abraham was reserved for the Messianic dispensation now begun.

Now the promises were made to Abraham and his seed — several promises were made to Abraham; but the chief, repeated several times, was that of the blessing through Christ. He saith not, And to seeds, as of many — as if the promise were made to several kinds of seed. But as of one — one kind of seed, one posterity, one kind of sons. Which is Christ — including all that believe in him. Gen 22:18.

To him and his posterity. Were the promises made. He does not use the plural term, as if the promise extended to all descendants generally; he uses the singular, which Paul takes to be a specific reference to the Messiah. This is the seed — Christ — in whom all the nations of the earth are to be blessed, and through whom the promise reaches its ultimate fulfillment.

17.synthesis: The law, coming 430 years after the covenant with Abraham, cannot annul the promise already confirmed by God. The chronological argument is decisive: a document already sealed cannot be affected by a later codicil from a different hand. Ellicott states the inference directly from verse 15: the covenant with Abraham was sealed; the law came after; therefore the law cannot affect the covenant. Calvin addresses the objection from Origen, Jerome, and the Papists — that the sacramental ceremonies were given to preserve faith, not to add conditions — by showing that Paul's argument comprehends whatever interferes with a free promise, not ceremonies alone. Clarke provides the counting: 430 years from when the promise was first made to Abraham in Genesis 12:2-3. Wesley makes the temporal argument most crisply: the covenant was confirmed of God by the promise itself, by its repetition, and by solemn oath — all before Moses. Barnes specifies what "confirmed" means: the solemn promise God made to Abraham, ratified multiple times, is the prior document that the law as a subsequent instrument cannot touch.

voices.html: If we listen to Origen and Jerome, there will be little difficulty in refuting this argument. But Paul reasons: "A promise was given to Abraham four hundred and thirty years before the publication of the law; therefore the law which came after could not disannul the promise." Who will dare to explain this as applying to ceremonies alone, while Paul comprehends under it whatever interferes with a free promise?

The fulfilment of the promise is to be seen in the Messianic dispensation now begun. The Law, which was given four hundred and thirty years after the promise, had no power to cancel it. This verse contains the direct inference from the argument stated in Galatians 3:15: when a document has been sealed, no subsequent addition can affect it.

Confirmed before of God in Christ — the promise of justification made to believers in Christ Jesus, who are the spiritual seed. Four hundred and thirty years after — counting from the time when the promise was first made to Abraham in Gen 12:2,3. The law cannot disannul so as to make the promise of no effect; the prior covenant stands.

And this I say — what I mean is this. The covenant which was before confirmed of God — by the promise itself, by its repetition, and by a solemn oath — concerning the blessing of all nations. Through Christ, the law which was four hundred and thirty years after doth not disannul, so as to make the promise of no effect — neither with regard to the Gentiles nor with regard to Israel.

18.synthesis: The logic is pressed to its conclusion: inheritance and law are incompatible categories. If the inheritance is by the law, it is no longer by promise — but God gave it to Abraham by promise. The two modes — free gift and contractual obligation — cannot operate simultaneously on the same object. Calvin identifies the opponents' move that this verse counters: they would say they intended not to weaken the covenant but only to add the ceremonies required alongside it. Paul forecloses this: he comprehends under his argument whatever interferes with a free promise. Ellicott states the incompatibility of the two categories most clearly: law and promise, contract and free gift, are mutually exclusive ideas. Barnes identifies the significance of the word "gave" — the inheritance was promised to Abraham as a free gift, and as a free gift the spiritual Canaan is thrown open to his spiritual descendants. Wesley adds a structural observation: this is a new argument from the nature of the transaction, not merely from its timing — the argument from time was that the law came 430 years after; the argument from nature is that free gift and earned wages are opposites.

voices.html: His opponents might reply that nothing was farther from their intention than to weaken or disannul God's covenant. To deprive them of every kind of subterfuge, he comes forward with the assertion that salvation by the law and salvation by the promise of God are wholly inconsistent with each other. Who will dare to explain this as applying to ceremonies alone, while Paul comprehends under it whatever interferes with a free promise?

The fulfilment of the promise is unaffected by the Law. For it is not dependent upon the Law, or upon the Law and the promise combined, but upon the promise alone. Law and promise — in other words, contract and free gift — are incompatible ideas. The land of Canaan was promised to Abraham as a free gift, and as a free gift the spiritual Canaan is thrown open to his spiritual descendants.

The inheritance promised to Abraham — that he should be heir of the world (Rom 4:13). To that inheritance Paul refers here: it is an essential part of it that it comes by virtue of the promise, not by fulfilling the law. The word "gave" is important: it was a free gift, not a contract contingent on performance.

And again — this is a new argument. The former was drawn from the time, this from the nature, of the transaction. If the eternal inheritance be obtained by keeping the law, it is no more by virtue of the free promise — these being just opposite to each other. But it is by promise. Therefore it is not by the law.

19.synthesis: The obvious objection: if the law cannot justify and cannot affect the promise, why was it given at all? The answer is that it was added because of transgressions, given through angels by a mediator, and destined to last only until the seed should come. This verse is theologically rich and interpretively complex. Wesley makes a distinctive two-part reading: the ceremonial law was inflicted as a divine punishment for the national sin of the golden calf (Exodus 32), while the moral law was added to discover and restrain transgressions and give some check to sin. Calvin reads "because of transgressions" more broadly — to convince men of guilt, to act as a curb on external behavior, and to point them to the promise. Clarke similarly reads it as giving knowledge of sin through a rule the people clearly violated. Barnes identifies the most pressing difficulty with the Judaisers' position: if the law was given precisely to address transgressions — to make sin visible, to restrain it, to point beyond it — then it was never designed to be the way of righteousness, which further confirms it is not now the way of righteousness. All sources agree the law was temporary in design — "till the seed should come" — and that its angels-mediator promulgation contrasts with the directness of the promise.

voices.html: When we are told that the law has no influence in obtaining justification, various suggestions immediately arise — that it must be useless, or opposed to God's covenant. He answers: the law was given because of transgressions — to make them manifest, to hold them in check, to convince men of guilt and need. It was a parenthesis in the design of Providence, added temporarily, destined to last only until the seed came.

Wherefore then is the Law? — literally, What then is the Law? What is its object or function? The Apostle proceeds to answer. It was added — not a part of the original scheme, but a sort of marginal addition; a parenthesis in the design of Providence. The direct line of God's dealings with man ran through the promise and its fulfillment in Christ.

It — the ceremonial law. Was added — to the promise. Because of transgressions — probably the yoke of the ceremonial law was inflicted as a punishment for the national sin of idolatry (Exod 32:1), at least the more grievous parts of it; and the whole was a prophetic type of Christ. The moral law was added to the promise to discover and restrain transgressions and give some check to sin; and this law passeth not away — but the ceremonial law was to last only till the seed should come.

This is obviously an objection which might be urged to the apostle's reasoning. Of what use was the law? Why were there so many wonderful exhibitions of divine power at its promulgation? Paul shows that its purpose was never to be an alternative route to justification — it was given because of transgressions, to make them visible and to restrain them, and it was always destined to end when the promised seed came.

19.key_tension: Wesley distinguishes two layers: the ceremonial law added as divine punishment for golden-calf idolatry, and the moral law added to reveal and restrain sin generally; Calvin and Barnes read the purpose more unitarily as exposing sin and pointing beyond it to the promise, without this two-layer distinction.

20.synthesis: "A mediator is not a mediator of one party, but God is one." This verse has been acknowledged by every commentator in the tradition as extraordinarily difficult. Barnes quotes Bloomfield calling it "the most perplexing verse in the New Testament." The basic logic all sources agree on: a mediator implies at least two parties with conditions to be observed by both; therefore the law, given through a mediator, is a conditional arrangement. The promise, by contrast, was given by God alone — one party, acting in pure sovereign grace, with no conditions contingent on human performance. Calvin reads the argument as showing that there was no room for a mediator in the Abrahamic promise — God alone acted, so no conditions from a second party applied. Wesley makes the cleanest statement of the two-parties logic: a mediator must be a middle person between two; in the free promise to Abraham God is only one of the parties, and the other (Abraham) was not present at the time of Moses; therefore in the law Moses's mediation is irrelevant to the promise. Clarke acknowledges the verse is both obscure and difficult and that learned men disagree considerably. Given this genuine diversity in interpretation among these sources, the key point remains: the promise depends on God alone, while the law depended on both parties, and this asymmetry confirms the promise's unconditional permanence.

voices.html: Some are disposed to philosophize on this expression, but that Paul is here speaking of the contracting parties, no man of sound judgment entertains a doubt. There is no room for a Mediator unless when one of the parties has a matter to transact with the other. The point is that the promise was given by God alone — one party — and no human performance constitutes a second party's condition for its fulfillment.

The mention of the word "mediator" implies a contract to which there are at least two parties. But where there is a contract there must be conditions, and if these conditions are not observed the whole falls to the ground. Such was the Law. But the promise depends upon God alone. He gave it, and he will assuredly keep it, no matter what man may do. God alone is concerned in it.

Now the mediator is not a mediator of one — there must be two parties, or there can be no mediator between them; but God who made the free promise to Abraham is only one of the parties. The other, Abraham, was not present at the time of Moses. Therefore in the promise Moses had nothing to do. The law, wherein he was concerned, was a transaction of quite another nature.

A mediator is not a mediator of one — as a mediator, mesites, signifies a middle person, there must necessarily be two parties between whom he stands, and acts in reference to both. This verse is allowed to be both obscure and difficult; and it is certain that there is little consent among learned men and critics in their opinions concerning it. The core point is that promise and law operate on different logics entirely.

20.key_tension: All sources agree on the underlying point (promise vs. law operate on different logics), but their explanations of verse 20 itself differ: Calvin focuses on the single-party nature of the promise; Wesley emphasizes Abraham's absence from the Sinai transaction; Clarke acknowledges genuine scholarly division; Ellicott focuses on the conditionality that mediation implies.

21.synthesis: Is the law then against the promises of God? Paul's emphatic answer is no — the two systems are well consistent, but they serve different functions. Calvin reads this as perhaps an exclamation of relief: dreading no further contradiction, Paul concludes that his previous arguments have placed it beyond doubt that the law cannot be opposed to faith while yet being consistent with God's purpose. Ellicott notes the argument begins with a concession: though the law was no substitute for the promise, it yet directly led up to it. The hypothetical — "if a law had been given that could give life, then righteousness would indeed be by the law" — shows that the problem with the law is not its character but its incapacity: righteousness is not an impossible aspiration, only an impossible attainment by law. Wesley makes the logical point tightest: if any law or rule of life could save sinners, God would have spared his own Son — the very necessity of Christ's death proves the law's incapacity. Barnes identifies the apostle's design: to deny in the most positive manner any contradiction between law and promise, then show what the law's actual design was — to further the plan contemplated in the promise, not to replace it. Clarke: the promise was not nullified by the law but presupposed it — the law showed the need, the promise supplied the answer.

voices.html: The certainty and steadiness of the divine purpose being admitted, we are bound equally to conclude that its results are not contrary to each other. Paul concludes that the former arguments have placed it beyond a doubt that the law cannot be opposed to faith — both are of God, both serve his purpose, but they serve different functions and neither duplicates the other.

The argument which follows begins with a concession. Though the Law was no substitute for the promise, it yet directly led up to it. For if a law could have given life — this is practically equivalent to "justified" or "made righteous." He who is justified has life — both true spiritual life now, and eternal life hereafter. If law could do this, righteousness would have been by the law.

Will it follow hence that the law is against, opposite to, the promises of God? By no means. They are well consistent. But yet the law cannot give life, as the promise doth. If there had been a law which could have given life — which could have entitled a sinner to life, God would have spared his own Son, and righteousness with all the blessings consequent upon it would have been by that law.

Is the law of Moses to be regarded as opposed to the promises made to Abraham? The object of the apostle in asking this question is evidently to take an opportunity to deny, in the most positive manner, that there can be any such clashing. He shows what was the design of the law and declares that the object was to further the plan contemplated in the promise, not to be an alternative to it.

22.synthesis: Scripture — meaning the law in particular — has shut up all under sin, so that the promise by faith in Jesus Christ might be given to those who believe. The imprisoning work of the law is thus revealed as purposive: the lock-up creates the condition under which the gift of grace becomes the only possible exit. Ellicott gives the most precise lexical treatment: the word translated "hath concluded" means to shut up, hem in, prevent from straying either to the right or left — as a shepherd shuts his flock in the fold. Calvin reads it as the law taking away righteousness from all, which is the most powerful kind of argument: you seek righteousness in the law, but the law itself with all of Scripture leaves nothing to men but condemnation. Clarke identifies the specific content: all the writings of the prophets uniformly declared that men are sinners, and the law declares the same through its continual sacrifices. The image of being locked up in a prison until sentence is pronounced is vivid — Barnes uses it: declared all men sinners, they cannot be justified by that law which condemns them any more than a murderer can be acquitted by the law that holds him guilty. Wesley identifies the purpose: all being cut off from expecting justification by the law, the promise might be freely given to those who believe.

voices.html: By the word Scripture is chiefly intended the law itself. It "hath concluded all under sin," and therefore, instead of giving, it takes away righteousness from all. The reasoning is most powerful: "You seek righteousness in the law; but the law itself, with the whole of Scripture, leaves nothing to men but condemnation; for all men, with their works, are pronounced to be unrighteous."

Hath concluded — the same peculiar word occurs in Romans 11:32 with a similar sense. It means to "shut up," "hem in," "prevent from straying either to the right hand or to the left," as a shepherd shuts up his flock in the fold. All is put in the neuter gender only to give more complete universality — what is meant is all mankind.

But the scripture hath concluded — all the writings of the prophets have uniformly declared that men are all sinners, and the law declares the same by the continual sacrifices which it prescribes. All therefore have sinned; and, being tried and found guilty, synekleisen he graphe, the Scripture hath shut them up — put them in prison and locked them up, till the time should come for the sentence of the law to be superseded by the grace of the Gospel.

But, on the contrary, the scripture wherein that law is written hath concluded all under sin — hath shut them up together (so the word properly signifies), as in a prison, under sentence of death, to the end that all being cut off from expecting justification by the law, the promise might be freely given to them that believe.

23.synthesis: Before faith came — before the gospel dispensation began — we were held under the law, locked up in custody, until the faith that was to be revealed arrived. The verse extends the prison metaphor of verse 22: the law was both the warden and the cell, keeping those under it in a condition of guarded captivity. Ellicott articulates the function clearly: it was a custody from which there was no permitted escape, a stern guardian that had to be obeyed. Calvin explains the pedagogical logic: if the law was always temporary, the diversity of governance between Jew and Gentile is explained — the Jews were kept in this special custody precisely because they were to be recipients of the fuller revelation when it came. Clarke keeps it simple: before the Gospel was published, we were kept as in a stronghold, synkekleismenoi, locked up, unto the faith — the religion of the Lord Jesus — which should afterward be revealed. Wesley identifies the two referents: "before faith came" means before the gospel dispensation; "kept under the law" means under the Mosaic dispensation; "shut up unto the faith" means reserved and prepared for the gospel. Barnes specifies that "before faith" denotes the Christian religion as such, because faith is its distinguishing characteristic, and the whole period prior to Christ was a period of being under the law's custodial custody.

voices.html: Before faith came — before faith awoke into exercise, began to exist, or the preaching of Christ as its object. We were kept in ward — so as to bring out more clearly the force of the metaphor which runs through the verse. The Law was a kind of prison-house in which we were kept shut up. It was a custody from which we were not permitted to escape — a stern guardian that we were made to obey, unto the faith which was to be revealed.

The question proposed is now more fully defined. He explains at great length the use of the law and the reason why it was temporal; for otherwise it would have appeared always unreasonable that a law should be delivered to the Jews from which the Gentiles were excluded. If there be but one church of Jews and Gentiles, why is there a diversity in its government? Whence is this new liberty derived, and on what authority does it rest?

But before faith came — before the Gospel was published. We were kept under the law, shut up — Ephrouroumetha — we were kept as in a strong hold, synkekleismenoi, locked up, unto the faith — the religion of the Lord Jesus, which should afterwards be revealed. Here the same metaphor is used as above: imprisoned, awaiting the amnesty that faith would bring.

But before faith — that is, the gospel dispensation — came, we were kept — as in close custody. Under the law — the Mosaic dispensation. Shut up unto the faith which was to be revealed — reserved and prepared for the gospel dispensation.

24.synthesis: The law was our pedagogue — our schoolmaster or guardian — leading us to Christ. The Greek paidagogos was not the teacher but the slave or freedman who had charge of a child and conducted him to school; Ellicott and Barnes both emphasize this distinction: the pedagogus was a moral disciplinarian and escort, not an instructor. Calvin gives the second comparison: as the law was earlier compared to a guardian, here it is likened to the paidagogos, who was usually a freedman or slave who conducted children to school, kept order, and performed other offices of a domestic nature. Clarke explains the practical implication: the law did not teach us the living, saving knowledge; by its rites, ceremonies, and especially sacrifices, it directed us to Christ. Wesley identifies the dual means by which the law served as escort to Christ: its commands showed the need we had of his atonement; its ceremonies all pointed us to him. Barnes notes the temporary nature of the role by definition — the pedagogue's charge ended when the child reached full age; similarly, the law's custodial function ended when Christ came. Though sources agree on the main point, they differ on which aspect of the pedagogus metaphor is primary — moral discipline vs. escort to the teacher vs. ceremonial pointing — making the consensus mixed on emphasis.

voices.html: A schoolmaster is not appointed for the whole life, but only for childhood, as the etymology of the Greek word paidagogos implies. The law was before compared to a guardian, and is now likened to a paidagogos — a person (usually a freedman or slave) who conducted children to school, kept order, and performed other domestic offices. The role is temporary and preparatory by definition.

Not quite a satisfactory translation, yet it is difficult to suggest a better. The Greek word is from which is derived the English "pedagogue." Originally it meant the slave placed in charge of a child, whose duty was to conduct it to school. The idea is of moral rather than intellectual discipline. The care of the pedagogus ceased where that of the schoolmaster began, but it was he who had more especially to form the moral character of the child.

The law was our pedagogue unto Christ. The paidagogos is not the schoolmaster, but the servant who had the care of the children to lead them to and from school, and who had the care of them out of school hours. The law did not teach us the living, saving knowledge; but, by its rites and ceremonies, and especially by its sacrifices, it directed us to Christ in whom alone that saving knowledge is found.

The word rendered schoolmaster, paidagogos, referred originally to a slave or freedman to whose care boys were committed, and who accompanied them to the public schools. The idea here is not that of instructor but of the office of the paedagogus — not teaching but accompanying, guarding, and preparing. The office was temporary: when the child arrived at the proper age he was released.

24.key_tension: All sources agree the law functioned as a temporary custodian leading to Christ, but differ on the primary mode: Calvin emphasizes the temporary/childhood aspect; Clarke and Wesley emphasize the ceremonies pointing to Christ; Ellicott and Barnes emphasize the moral-disciplinarian/escort role rather than the teaching role.

25.synthesis: Now that faith has come, we are no longer under the pedagogue. The schoolmaster's reign is over; the age of minority has ended. Calvin reads this as the brighter revelation of grace after the veil of the temple was rent — the manifestation of Christ marked the end of childhood which needed to be placed under a schoolmaster. Clarke specifies that "the faith" means the whole of the Mosaic economy in its function of pointing forward, and "the coming of faith" means the full manifestation of Christ in the Gospel: when Christ was manifested and the Gospel preached, the believers came to Christ, learned of him, became wise unto salvation, and had their fruit unto holiness. Wesley is briefest: faith — that is, the gospel dispensation — being come, we are no longer under that schoolmaster, the Mosaic dispensation. Barnes explains the practical consequence: no longer kept in restraint and under bondage, no longer led along to another to receive instruction — now directly under the great Teacher himself, with a kind of freedom that was not allowed before. Ellicott's entry is at verse 26, having covered the verse under verse 24; the transition is from custodial minority to adult sonship, which verse 26 will specify.

voices.html: This phrase denotes the brighter revelation of grace after that the veil of the temple was rent in twain (Matt 27:51), which was effected by the manifestation of Christ. He affirms that under the reign of Christ there is no longer any childhood which needs to be placed under a schoolmaster, and that consequently the law has resigned its office — which is another application of the comparison already employed.

But, after that faith is come — when Christ was manifested in the flesh, and the Gospel was preached, we were no longer under the pedagogue; we came to Christ, learned of him, became wise unto salvation, had our fruit unto holiness, and the end eternal life. As the Law signifies the whole Mosaic economy, so the Faith signifies the whole Christian economy.
But faith — that is, the gospel dispensation. Being come, we are no longer under that schoolmaster — the Mosaic dispensation.

The scheme of salvation by faith. After that is revealed. Gal 3:23. We are no longer under a schoolmaster — under the pedagogus. We are not kept in restraint and under bondage, and led along to another to receive instruction. We are directly under the great Teacher, the Instructor himself; and have a kind of freedom which we were not allowed before. The bondage has passed away; we are free from the burdensome ceremonies.

26.synthesis: For you are all sons of God through faith in Christ Jesus. The transition from the schoolmaster metaphor is exact: Ellicott notes that the translation "children" here is unfortunate — the point is precisely that the Christian is no longer in the condition of children under a pedagogue, but has the status of grown-up sons with the full privileges of sonship. The pre-Messianic period was childhood/minority; the Messianic period is full adulthood in the Father's house. Calvin makes the logical connection: it would be unjust and unreasonable for the law to hold believers in perpetual slavery, for they are the children of God — and the fact of being children proves their freedom. Clarke reduces it to its essence: believers who have received the Gospel are all children of God by faith in Christ Jesus, not by circumcision or any Mosaic observance. Wesley's comment is similarly compact: Christians are adult sons of God, and so need a schoolmaster no longer. Barnes draws on John 1:12 as the fullest statement of what it means to be children of God: received him, to them gave he power to become the sons of God, even to them that believe on his name — the same condition of faith, the same resulting status.

voices.html: It would be unjust, and in the highest degree unreasonable, that the law should hold believers in perpetual slavery. This is proved by the additional argument that they are the children of God. It would not be enough to say that we are no longer children, unless it were added that we are freemen; for in slaves age makes no alteration. The fact of their being the children of God proves their freedom.

The translation "children" here is unfortunate, as the point to be brought out is that the Christian is no longer in the condition of "children," but in that of grown-up "sons." The pre-Messianic period bears to the Messianic period the same relation that childhood or minority bears to full age. The Christian has the privileges of an adult son in his Father's house, released from pupilage and having received his freedom.

For ye, who have believed the Gospel, are all the children of God by faith in Christ Jesus — but no man is a child of God by circumcision, nor by any observance of the Mosaic law. The basis of sonship is entirely changed from ethnic lineage and ritual observance to faith in Christ.

All who bear the Christian name — the converts from among the Jews and Gentiles alike. The idea here is that they are no longer under tutors and governors, no longer subject to the direction and will of the paedagogus; they are arrived at age and are admitted to the privileges of sons. John 1:12.

27.synthesis: For as many as have been baptized into Christ have put on Christ. The reason why all believers are sons of God is their union with Christ through baptism: they are clothed with him, sharing his identity and standing. Calvin employs the metaphor of a garment — not that believers are externally clothed and internally unchanged, but that they are so completely incorporated into Christ that what belongs to him is communicated to them. Ellicott distinguishes "baptized into Christ" from merely "baptized in the name of Christ": the former implies the contracting of a very close and intimate relation, the nature of which is expressed by "put on Christ." Clarke explains the cultural background: to put on or to be clothed with one is to assume the person and character of that one — those who do so are bound to act his part. Barnes draws the parallel from Romans 13:14 ("put on the Lord Jesus Christ") and explains the phrase as assuming the sentiments, opinions, and characteristic traits of Christ, as a man clothes himself. Wesley is most compressed: as many who have testified their faith by being baptized in the name of Christ have put on Christ — have received him as their righteousness and are therefore sons of God through him.

voices.html: The greater and loftier the privilege is of being the children of God, the farther is it removed from our senses, and the more difficult to obtain belief. He therefore explains what is implied in our being united — or rather made one — with the Son of God; so as to remove all doubt that what belongs to him is communicated to us. He employs the metaphor of a garment: the Galatians have clothed themselves with Christ.

For. This introduces the reason why the Christian stands to God in the relation of an adult son. Baptized into Christ — to be baptised "into" Christ is something more than merely "to be baptised in the name of Christ." It implies the contracting of a very close and intimate relation, the nature of which is expressed in the phrase which follows: have put on Christ.

As many of you as have been baptized into Christ — all of you who have believed in Christ as the promised Messiah, and received baptism as a public proof that ye had received Christ as your Lord and Savior, have put on Christ — have received his Spirit, and entered into his interests, and copied his manners. To put on or to be clothed with one is to assume the person and character of that one.

Whether by nature Jews or Gentiles. As have been baptized into Christ — or unto Christ — baptized with reference to him, or receiving him as the Savior. Have put on Christ — that is, they have put on his sentiments, opinions, characteristic traits, as a man clothes himself. Romans 13:14.

28.synthesis: There is neither Jew nor Greek, slave nor free, male nor female — for you are all one in Christ Jesus. The unity in Christ dissolves every social and ethnic distinction as it relates to standing before God. Calvin identifies the main point: there is no distinction of persons here, and it is therefore of no consequence to what nation or condition any one belongs — circumcision is no more regarded than sex or civil rank, because Christ makes them all one. Ellicott emphasizes the comprehensiveness and the solidarity: all Christians alike, no matter what their race, status, or sex, stand on the same footing of sonship before God. There is a unity in the Christian body such that what is true of one is true of all. Clarke notes that "Greek" in the Jewish idiom means Gentile generally — the whole world was divided into Jews and Greeks — and gives particular attention to "neither male nor female": with great reason Paul introduces this, because circumcision had put a specific distinction between the sexes under the old dispensation, and in Christ this distinction in access to God is explicitly removed. Barnes makes the social scope plain: no favoritism on account of birth, beauty, or blood — all confess themselves sinners, all saved by the same Savior, all admitted to the same privileges as children of God. Wesley connects this specifically to circumcision's removal: circumcision being laid aside, which was peculiar to males, the distinction it created in the dispensation between Jews and Gentiles is gone.

voices.html: The meaning is that there is no distinction of persons here, and therefore it is of no consequence to what nation or condition any one may belong — nor is circumcision any more regarded than sex or civil rank. And why? Because Christ makes them all one. Whatever may have been their former differences, Christ alone is able to unite them all. The apostle's object is to show that the grace of adoption and the hope of salvation are not confined to the Jews.

This verse continues the proof that all Christians are in the fullest sense sons of God. All Christians alike, no matter what their race, status, or sex, stand on the same footing of sonship before God. There is a unity or solidarity in the Christian body. What is true of one is true of all. The spread of the Greek race through Alexander's conquests had made "Greek" almost synonymous with "Gentile" to the Jewish mind.

There is neither Jew nor Greek — Hellen, Greek, is put here for ethnikos, heathen. Under the Gospel all distinctions are done away. Neither male nor female — with great reason the apostle introduces this; for circumcision, being peculiar to males, put a difference between the sexes, during that dispensation, in regard to access to God. In Christ, this distinction is explicitly removed.

All are on a level; all are saved in the same way; all are entitled to the same privileges. There is no favoritism on account of birth, beauty, or blood. All confess that they are sinners; all are saved by the merits of the same Saviour; all are admitted to the same privileges as children of God. The word "Greek" is used to denote the Gentiles generally; the whole world was divided by the Jews into "Jews and Greeks."

29.synthesis: The conclusion of the whole argument: if you are Christ's, then you are Abraham's offspring and heirs according to the promise. Calvin identifies the purpose: not to suggest that being Abraham's child is better than being a member of Christ — Christ is not reduced to the level of Abraham — but to repress the pride of the Jews who gloried in their lineage as if they alone were the people of God. The very distinction they prized most — descent from Abraham — is made common to all who believe in Christ. Ellicott reads the verse as the logical terminus: the followers of the Messiah are the true seed of Abraham, and the kingdom of the Messiah, which they possess, is the promised inheritance. Barnes draws the theological consequence: if believers in Christ are interested in the work of the Messiah, they show that they are the spiritual descendants of Abraham — nationality makes no difference, since what mattered in Abraham was not his ethnicity but his faith, and those who share that faith share the standing. Clarke includes the eschatological dimension: being heirs, they are fitted for the rest that remains for the people of God, the heavenly inheritance typified by Canaan. Wesley reduces it to the essential hinge: "if ye are Christ's" — that is, believers in him — then "ye are Abraham's seed." Faith in Christ is the only passport into the Abrahamic inheritance.

voices.html: This is not intended to convey the idea that to be a child of Abraham is better than to be a member of Christ — but to repress the pride of the Jews, who gloried in their privilege as if they alone were the people of God. They reckoned no distinction higher than to belong to the race of Abraham; and this very distinction he makes to be common to all who believe in Christ. The conclusion rests on Christ being the blessed seed through whom all nations were to be blessed.

Conclusion of the whole argument. The followers of the Messiah are the true seed of Abraham. The kingdom of the Messiah, which they possess, is the promised inheritance. All the strands of the chapter — the experience argument, the Abraham argument, the promise-and-law argument, the pedagogue — converge here.

If you belong to the Messiah, and are interested in his work, then are ye Abraham's seed. The promise made to Abraham related to the Messiah. Abraham believed in that Messiah, and was distinguished for his faith in him who was to come. No matter whether they were Jews or Gentiles, whether circumcised or not — the spiritual descendants of Abraham are those who share his faith in the promised seed.

If ye are Christ's — that is, believers in him. Then are ye Abraham's seed — that is, ye are that real, spiritual posterity of Abraham to whom the promises were made; and then heirs, according to that promise, being fitted for the rest that remains for the people of God, that heavenly inheritance which was typified by the earthly Canaan.

1.synthesis: The chapter opens with Paul's imperative to stand firm in the freedom Christ has purchased. Calvin frames this as an invaluable blessing worth defending even to death: not merely a temporal benefit but an eternal one, since the spiritual life itself is at stake. Ellicott notes a textual variant in the best manuscripts reading with freedom Christ made us free, making liberty both the instrument and purpose of his liberating act. Barnes emphasizes that this is freedom from Jewish rites and ceremonies, while Wesley specifies freedom from the ceremonial law's burden. Robertson underscores the perfect stem of stekete: keep on standing, stay free since Christ set you free. Clarke reads the yoke as the entire Mosaic economy imposed on Gentiles. The command is not passive; it requires active resistance against re-entanglement. Paul does not call believers to avoid bondage but to maintain a freedom already granted. All agree that Christian liberty is not license but liberation for a higher life under the Spirit.

voices.html: It is an invaluable blessing, in defense of which it is our duty to fight, even to death; since not only the highest temporal considerations, but our eternal interests also, animate us to the contest. The apostle now reminds them that they ought not lightly to despise a freedom so precious.

Stand fast therefore uses the perfect stem of histemi: keep on standing therefore, stay free since Christ set you free. The dative case in te eleutheria reads for freedom, for the freedom that belongs to us as children of the freewoman.

Stand fast in the liberty from the ceremonial law wherewith Christ hath made us all believers free; and be not entangled again with the yoke of legal bondage which has no power to justify but only to burden.

2.synthesis: Paul asserts his full apostolic authority to declare that circumcision sought as a means of justification nullifies one's relationship with Christ. Ellicott observes the strong personality of the apostle here, speaking with dogmatic authority as though his bare word were sufficient. Calvin puzzles over whether Timothy's circumcision offers a counterexample and resolves it by insisting Paul's argument concerns circumcision as a condition of justification, not circumcision as such. Barnes carefully distinguishes: Paul himself was circumcised but not to be justified thereby, and that distinction is decisive. Wesley is blunt: seeking justification by circumcision means disclaiming Christ and all his blessings. Robertson notes the present passive subjunctive as a supposable case with terrible consequences, for it would make circumcision a condition of salvation and thus render Christ useless. The apostolic ego of verse 2 carries the full weight of Paul's founding authority over these churches, making the declaration all the more urgent.

voices.html: The strong personality of the Apostle asserts itself; instead of going into an elaborate proof, he speaks with dogmatic authority, as though his bare word were enough. He says that if this state of being received into divine favour is sought through circumcision, Christ will profit nothing.

I, who at first preached the gospel to you; I, too, who have been circumcised, and who was formerly a strenuous asserter of the necessity of observing the laws of Moses, now solemnly say to you that if you are circumcised with a view to being justified by that, it amounts to a rejection of justification by Christ.

Condition of third class and present passive subjunctive: a supposable case, but with terrible consequences, for they will make circumcision a condition of salvation. In that case Christ will help them not at all.

3.synthesis: Whoever accepts circumcision as part of the salvific system incurs obligation to keep the entire Mosaic code. Calvin cites Grotius to sharpen the logic: if Judaism is the road to salvation, the whole of Judaism must be observed; you cannot cull and discard whatever portions seem inconvenient. Since no one will ever satisfy the law completely, circumcision as a means of justification leads only to perpetual condemnation. Barnes notes that circumcision was the distinguishing badge of the Jews, and accepting it committed one to the whole legal system in the same way baptism commits one to Christ. Clarke emphasizes the impossibility of partial compliance: the system is indivisible. Robertson is terse but precise, observing that the debtor has assumed an obligation and thereby takes the curse upon himself. Wesley addresses it directly to Gentile hearers: he thereby makes himself a debtor and obliges himself to the whole law which no flesh can satisfy.

voices.html: If Judaism is the road to salvation, the whole of Judaism must be observed; you must not cull and throw away whatever part of it you think fit. He will never escape death, but will always continue to be held as guilty, for no man will ever be found who satisfies the law.

He binds himself to obey all the law of Moses. Circumcision was the distinguishing badge of the Jews, as baptism is of Christians. A man who was circumcised was, by that act, committed to the whole Jewish system of religion and could not pick and choose among its requirements.
Opheiletes: a common word for one who has assumed an obligation. See on Matthew 6:12, Galatians 3:10. He takes the curse upon himself by binding himself to a system no one can fully satisfy.

4.synthesis: Paul's verdict on those who seek justification through the law is stark: Christ is become of no effect to you, and you have fallen from grace. The Galatians were not, as Calvin observes, so grossly mistaken as to believe the law alone justified them; they attempted to mix Christ with the law. But any dilution of grace with merit cuts off the channel through which grace flows. Ellicott renders it as ye did fall out of grace, a decisive completed action. Barnes explains that the Greek verb katargeo means to render inactive, idle, useless, treating Christ's work as needless and vain. Wesley frames it as renouncing the new covenant and disclaiming the benefit of the gracious dispensation. Robertson notes the second aorist active indicative of ekpipto with the ablative: they fell out from grace as from a vessel they once inhabited. The combination of being severed from Christ and having fallen from grace is total spiritual displacement.

voices.html: They were not so grossly mistaken as to believe that by the observance of the law alone they were justified, but attempted to mix Christ with the law. What are you doing? You deprive yourselves of every advantage from Christ, and treat his grace as if it were of no value.

You will derive no advantage from Christ. His work in regard to you is needless and vain. If you can be justified in any other way than by him, then of course you do not need him, and your adoption of the other mode is, in fact, a renunciation of him.

Kataergeo: first aorist passive, to make null and void. Ye are fallen away from grace: second aorist active of ekpipto with ablative case. Ye did fall out of grace, ye left the grace-sphere where you once lived.

5.synthesis: In deliberate contrast to those who seek righteousness through the law, Paul describes the Christian's true posture: we wait by the Spirit through faith for the hope of righteousness. Calvin explains that righteousness depends on faith alone, obtained through the Spirit without ceremonies. Barnes stresses that true Christians have no other hope of salvation than by faith in the Lord Jesus; this is not passive waiting for a delayed event but expectant confidence in the only means of justification. Wesley identifies the recipients as those under the gospel dispensation, who receive righteousness from God through faith and by faith will obtain the full reward. Robertson's compressed comment strikes at the contrast: we Christians as opposed to the legalists; by the Spirit out of faith, not law. Clear-cut repetition to make it plain. The phrase hope of righteousness captures both the present reality of justification by faith and its eschatological completion.

voices.html: Righteousness depends on faith, and is obtained, through the Spirit, without ceremonies. To wait for righteousness is to place our confidence in this or that object, to decide from what quarter righteousness is to be expected; and the words contain the exhortation, let us continue steadfastly in the hope of righteousness which God promises.

We expect salvation only by the aid of the Holy Spirit. The main idea is not that of waiting as if the thing were delayed; it is that of expecting. True Christians have no other hope of salvation than by faith in the Lord Jesus, not by works or conformity to the law.
We Christians as opposed to the legalists. By the Spirit, the Holy Spirit, out of faith, not law. Clear-cut repetition to make it plain that Spirit and faith stand over against law and circumcision.

6.synthesis: In Christ Jesus neither circumcision nor uncircumcision avails anything; only faith working through love. Calvin explains that circumcision and its appendages are abolished in the kingdom of Christ, the rite no longer possessing influence. Yet he insists this does not mean ceremonies were always useless, only that their day is past. Barnes sees the design of Christianity as abolishing rites that divided humanity and introducing a way of salvation applicable to all mankind alike. Wesley is precise: neither the most punctual observance of the law nor the most exact heathen morality avails anything; only faith alone, and that specifically a faith that works by love, covering all inward and outward holiness. Robertson calls faith working through love the moral dynamic of Paul's conception of freedom from law. Clarke notes that all distinctions of race and ritual dissolve before the one decisive question: has the Spirit of Christ been received by faith that expresses itself in love?

voices.html: In the kingdom of Christ, circumcision with its appendages is abolished; faith depends on the Spirit and not on ceremonies. While declaring they no longer possess any influence, Paul does not admit that they were always useless; for he does not condemn them in all ages, but only in the present time.

In Christ Jesus neither circumcision with the most punctual observance of the law, nor uncircumcision with the most exact heathen morality, availeth anything toward justification or salvation. But faith alone; even that faith which worketh by love, all inward and outward holiness.

Neither Jew nor Greek has any recommendation in his state. All stand on a level in Christ. Faith working through love: the middle voice of energeo, through love as the moral dynamic of Paul's conception of freedom from law.

7.synthesis: Paul uses the athletic metaphor of a race interrupted. They ran well; someone cut in on them. Calvin notes that the censure is mingled with approbation of their former course, designed to produce shame and thereby speed their return to the right path. Barnes recalls the context of the Greek Olympic games; the word for hindrance means to beat or drive back, or to obstruct someone running. Wesley is brief: who hath hindered you that ye should not still obey the truth? Robertson provides a vivid modern analogy: the Greek verb is like someone cutting in on your telephone call, a ringleader who cut across the Galatians' course as they were running the Christian race and tried to trip them or turn them. Ellicott and Clarke both observe the implied rebuke: the Galatians once ran faithfully under Paul's gospel and the departure from that course has an external cause, a seducer, not an internal one.

voices.html: The censure which the apostle administers for their present departure from the truth is mingled with approbation of their former course, for the express purpose that, by being brought to a sense of shame, they may return more speedily to the right path. The astonishment conveyed in the question was intended to produce a blush.

The Christian life is often represented as a race. Paul means here that they began the Christian life with ardour and zeal. The word used means, properly, to beat or drive back; an Olympic expression for coming across the course while a person is running, checking and retarding them.

Who did hinder you: first aorist active of enkoptoo, to cut in on one, for all the world like our use of one cutting in on us at the telephone. Note the singular tis. There was some ringleader who cut in on the Galatians as they were running the Christian race.

8.synthesis: The persuasion drawing the Galatians toward the law did not come from God who called them. Calvin argues that Paul's authority as the one who first announced their divine calling gives him standing to make this bold claim. Barnes emphasizes that the Galatians knew Paul had not persuaded them toward Judaizing, so the important demonstration is that such persuasion cannot be traced to God, however its teachers may claim divine sanction. Wesley reduces it to a single point: this persuasion cometh not from God who called you to his kingdom and glory. Robertson defines the rare word peismone as the art of persuasion, the effort of the Judaizers to persuade you, found only here and in later ecclesiastical writers. Clarke and Ellicott note the contrast between the caller and the persuader: God called them to freedom; whoever persuades them back toward the law acts against God's calling.

voices.html: Paul, to whom the Galatians had been indebted for the announcement of their Divine calling, was well entitled to address them in this confident language. He does not directly name its source, but pronounces that their present persuasion came not from God, though those who taught it may claim divine authority.

This refers to God who had called them into his kingdom. That it refers to God, and not to Paul, is plain. They knew well enough that Paul had not persuaded them to it, and it was important now to show them that it could not be traced to God.

He peismone: the art of persuasion, the effort of the Judaizers to persuade you. Only here and in ecclesiastical writers. The God who called you to freedom is not the source of any persuasion that leads back to bondage.

9.synthesis: A little leaven leavens the whole lump. Paul borrows this proverb, which he also uses in 1 Corinthians 5:6, to warn that partial corruption spreads through an entire community. Calvin explains Satan's characteristic stratagem: he does not attempt open destruction of the whole gospel but taints its purity by introducing corrupt opinions that seem minor. Many persons overlook what seems a slight error, not realizing how pervasive its consequences. Barnes sees two possible applications: either a pre-existing tendency among the Galatians to conform to rites, which has now like leaven leavened them, or the false teachers themselves compared to leaven working through the congregation. Robertson insists it is merely the pervasive power of leaven that is invoked here, not leaven as a symbol of evil, the same neutral point made in the parable of Matthew 13:33. Wesley applies it personally: one troubler troubles all.

voices.html: Satan's stratagem is that he does not attempt an avowed destruction of the whole gospel, but he taints its purity by introducing false and corrupt opinions. Many persons are thus led to overlook the seriousness, as they consider it of little moment, not perceiving how swiftly the contagion spreads.

This is evidently a proverbial expression. Its meaning here is that the embracing of the errors which they had adopted was to be traced to some influence existing among themselves, acting like leaven. Either a slight tendency to conform to rites has pervaded the mass, or the false teachers may be compared to leaven working through the whole lump.
Paul has this proverb also in 1 Corinthians 5:6. It is merely the pervasive power of leaven that is involved in the proverb, as in Matthew 13:33, not leaven as a symbol of evil in itself.

10.synthesis: Despite his sharp words, Paul expresses confidence in the Galatians that they will return to his mind on the matter, while pronouncing that whoever has troubled them will bear the judgment. Calvin notes that expressing good hopes about people gives them courage, for they reckon it shameful to disappoint those who are kind toward them. Yet the reversal of his fierceness against the false apostles is deliberate: all his anger is directed at the teachers, not the taught. Barnes reads Paul as believing that after they have heard his arguments they will again embrace his teaching. Wesley sees the singular troubler as one identifiable leader bearing a heavy burden already hanging over his head. Robertson observes that Paul uses indefinite relative clause form, suggesting he may not know the ringleader precisely, consistent with his use of singular tis in verse 7; judgment falls regardless of identity.

voices.html: It gives us courage to learn that good hopes are entertained about us; for we reckon it shameful to disappoint those whose feelings toward us are kind and friendly. But to the troublers all his fierceness is directed, and on them the punishment is threatened, while good hopes are expressed for the Galatians.

Though they had been led astray and had embraced many false opinions, yet, on the whole, Paul had confidence in their piety and believed they would yet return and embrace the truth. He seems to mean that after reading this letter they will be no otherwise minded than he is teaching them.

Yet I have confidence that after ye have read this, ye will be no otherwise minded than I am. But he that troubleth you shall bear his judgment, a heavy burden already hanging over his head, whoever he be that has led you astray.

11.synthesis: Paul refutes the slander that he still preaches circumcision, pointing to his ongoing persecution as proof of his consistent anti-circumcision gospel. Calvin argues: it would be completely in his power to avoid displeasure and danger were he only to mix ceremonies with Christ; the earnestness with which he opposes them is on no personal account. Barnes explains that false teachers may have appealed to Paul's circumcision of Timothy as evidence that he endorsed the practice for justification, but Paul's point is that if he truly preached circumcision he would not be persecuted. Wesley notes that Paul did not condemn condescending to weakness in others, but his doctrine was clear: the cross nullified the legal system. Robertson focuses on the theological connection: the offense of the cross was specifically the scandal that the crucified Christ had abolished the law, and that is precisely what Paul preaches.

voices.html: It would be completely in my power to avoid the displeasure of men and every kind of danger and persecution, were I only to mix ceremonies with Christ. The earnestness with which I oppose them is not on my own account nor for my own advantage; I oppose them because the gospel itself demands it.

Paul here proceeds to vindicate himself from giving countenance to the doctrines which the false teachers had advanced. It is evident they appealed to Paul himself and alleged he insisted on the necessity of circumcision. If he still preached circumcision, why is the offence of the cross not ceased and the persecution stopped?

The grand reason why the Jews so bitterly persecuted Paul was that his preaching Christ crucified implied the abolition of the law. If he preached circumcision that offence would cease. Yet he did not condemn conforming out of condescension to weakness; only his doctrine against circumcision for justification was clear.

12.synthesis: Paul's indignation reaches its sharpest point: he wishes those who are unsettling the Galatians would go further and castrate themselves. Most commentators read the cutting as a wish for excommunication from the church, with a bitter wordplay on circumcision, though Chrysostom and others read a harsher bodily meaning. Calvin sees the prayer as reflecting apostolic indignation at those who tear the church for the sake of circumcision, noting that Paul cannot be charged with cruelty since he elsewhere pleads for mercy toward the erring. Barnes explicitly rejects Chrysostom's interpretation as singular and monstrous, taking the meaning as exclusion from the communion of the church. Wesley agrees: cast out from your church those who thus trouble you. Robertson notes the late verb anastatountes meaning to disturb or drive from one's abode, and that Paul uses the future middle apokopsontai regarding those who unsettle them.

voices.html: His indignation proceeds still farther, and he prays for destruction on those impostors. The word cut off appears to be employed in allusion to the circumcision which they pressed: they tear the church for the sake of circumcision; I wish they were entirely cut off. Chrysostom favors the harsher reading, but Calvin believes apostolic mildness requires a different sense.

That is, as I understand it, from the communion of the church. So far am I from agreeing with them and preaching circumcision as they do, that I sincerely wish they were excluded from the church as unworthy a place among the children of God. The more monstrous interpretation adopted by Chrysostom, the learned reader may see refuted by Bishop Warburton.

I would they were even cut off from your communion; cast out of your church, that thus trouble you. The wordplay on circumcision sharpens the apostolic irony: those who insist on cutting should experience their own principle taken to its extreme.

12.key_tension: Commentators divide on whether cut off means excommunication from the church or carries a harsher bodily reference paralleling circumcision.

13.synthesis: Paul pivots: the Galatians have been called to liberty, but liberty must not become an occasion for the flesh. Calvin makes the important distinction between liberty and the use of liberty: liberty lies in the conscience before God; the use of it lies in outward conduct toward others. Having urged them to suffer no diminution of liberty, he now warns them not to exploit it. Barnes explains that the freedom Paul commends is freedom from sin and from bondage to Mosaic ceremony, and that using it as license for the flesh betrays both its source and its purpose.
