Clarke notes that in his pre-conversion life Paul courted the favor of the Sanhedrin and of Gamaliel; the transformation in his motive is itself evidence that something other than human calculation produced his message.

voices.html: Paul supports his assertion with two arguments. The first is that he was not prompted by ambition or love of approval. The very force of his language—cursing any who alter the gospel—is incompatible with a desire to please his hearers. A man seeking to win human favor does not open a letter by pronouncing anathema on those his readers might admire.

Persuade here means to conciliate, to seek favor with, to make friends of. The force of now is in speaking thus—the very act of pronouncing the anathema is Paul's answer. No one who speaks that way is trying to win human approval. The argument is that his conduct is its own refutation of the charge.

Paul adds the reason why he speaks so confidently. Do I now satisfy men?—Is this what I aim at in preaching? If I still pleased men as once I did, I could not be a servant of Christ. The servant of Christ must be willing to displease men when the truth requires it, and the anathema of the preceding verses is exactly that.

The word now expresses a contrast with Paul's former purpose of life. Before his conversion it was his object to conciliate human favor—he derived his standing from his peers in Judaism. Now his motive is entirely changed: he seeks the approval of God, and he is willing to say things that will win him enemies if God requires it.

11.synthesis:
With verse 11 Paul states the central thesis that will govern the rest of chapters 1 and 2: the gospel he preached is not according to man. It did not originate with human tradition, it was not shaped by human wisdom, and it was not delivered to him through any human teacher. This is the main hinge, as Calvin calls it—the pivot on which the entire argument turns. If Paul's gospel came from men, it can be revised by men; if it came from God, it cannot.

Clarke captures the force with characteristic directness: there is not a spark of human invention in it, nor the slightest touch of human cunning. Wesley notes the threefold denial: not from man (in its origin), not by man (in its transmission), not suited to the taste of man (in its content). Barnes notes that Paul had probably already made his readers aware of this when he was with them; he now states it as a formal declaration to be supported by the biographical evidence that follows.

voices.html: This is the most powerful argument—the main hinge on which the question turns: Paul has not received the gospel from men but it was revealed to him by God. As this might be denied, he offers a proof drawn from a narrative of facts. His autobiography is not self-promotion but evidence in a legal argument about the origin and authority of his message.

Paul wishes the Galatians fully to understand that the gospel he preached to them is not after man. There is not a spark of human invention in it, nor the slightest touch of human cunning. It bears no marks of having been constructed to suit any party interest or to please any human audience.

The gospel Paul preached was not according to man: not from man in its origin, not by man in its transmission, not suited to the natural taste of man in its content. The gospel crosses every human preference and expectation—it is precisely the kind of message that no one would have invented who was trying to please his audience.

12.synthesis:
The formal denial: I did not receive it from any man, nor was I taught it; but I received it through a revelation of Jesus Christ. Paul sets up a sharp contrast between two modes of transmission. Normal religious teaching passes through human chains—rabbi to student, tradition to tradition. Paul's gospel bypassed this chain entirely. He did not receive it (as a disciple receives from a teacher) nor was he taught it (as a student works through a body of doctrine). He received it through apocalypsis—unveiling, disclosure—of Jesus Christ himself.

Calvin notes that the false apostles attacked this specific point, claiming Paul had no direct commission and was dependent on the Twelve. The whole biographical section that follows is offered in proof against this charge. Clarke states that Paul was commissioned by Christ alone, receiving knowledge of the gospel from Christ himself. Wesley adds texture: Christ first revealed to Paul his resurrection and ascension and the calling of the Gentiles, then over time revealed other things not given in that first encounter. Barnes notes that this is specifically a response to opponents who had argued Paul derived his knowledge from the apostles in Jerusalem, since he had not been personally known to the Lord Jesus during his earthly ministry.

voices.html: The false apostles attacked Paul at this point, denying that his message came directly from God. He now offers proof from a narrative of facts. He did not merely assert divine origin—he shows it from the events of his life: his former enmity toward the gospel, his sudden conversion, his isolation from the apostles afterward, all testify that no human hand shaped what he preached.

Paul received the gospel by means of no apostle, as he had already stated at verse 1. No man taught him what he preached. He was commissioned by Christ alone, receiving the knowledge of it from Christ himself—not as a student who learned from a master but as a servant to whom the Lord directly entrusted his message.

Paul did not receive the gospel at once, nor was he taught it gradually by any man. Rather, Christ revealed to him his resurrection, ascension, and the calling of the Gentiles—and told him there were other things to be revealed in due time. The revelation was progressive but always direct, never mediated through human tradition.

This is said in reply to opponents who maintained that Paul had derived his knowledge of the gospel from other men, as he had not been personally known to the Lord Jesus nor numbered among the original Twelve. Paul's answer is that the revelation came directly: no human instruction stood between him and the risen Christ who commissioned him.

13.synthesis:
The autobiography begins with the most damaging fact: Paul was the gospel's enemy. He persecuted the church of God beyond measure and tried to destroy it. The word destroy is not hyperbole; Acts records his active participation in imprisonment, beatings, and death of believers. Paul does not soften this past. He presents it as evidence: a man who hated the gospel this intensely, and who was converted so completely, was not shaped by human persuasion or gradual intellectual development—he was changed by the power of God.

Calvin notes that the whole narrative was added as part of the argument: because Paul had been a mortal enemy of the gospel and a destroyer of the Christian name, it follows that his conversion and subsequent apostleship must have come from God alone. Barnes points out that Paul had probably already made them aware of these events, and the reference here is to show that his knowledge of the Christian religion did not come from the Jerusalem apostles—he had been doing everything in his power to annihilate what they stood for.

voices.html: Paul relates that during his whole former life he had such an abhorrence of the gospel that he was a mortal enemy of it and a destroyer of the name of Christianity. Hence we infer that his conversion must have come from above. No human persuasion could account for so complete a reversal; the power that produced it was divine.

Paul's manner of life before his conversion was one of extreme persecution. The verb wasted or destroyed conveys active, systematic effort to eradicate the church. This makes his subsequent apostleship all the more remarkable: the man who labored hardest to destroy the faith became the man who did most to extend it.

Paul refers to his former conduct to show that he had not derived his knowledge of the Christian religion from those who were apostles before him. He had been doing everything in his power to destroy what they proclaimed. The source of his gospel cannot have been the men he was persecuting; it could only have been the God who arrested him on the Damascus road.

14.synthesis:
Paul had not merely been a member of Judaism; he had been its champion, advancing beyond his contemporaries in zeal for the ancestral traditions. The word profited (προέκοπτον) means to cut forward, to advance rapidly—it suggests someone clearing a path through undergrowth. Paul outran his peers in ritualistic observance, in mastery of the oral tradition, and in competitive religious achievement. This background makes his abandonment of Judaism even more inexplicable by natural causes.

Ellicott notes that the progress implied is not merely in theological knowledge but in the width of Judaism itself—zeal for ritual observance, strictness of tradition, the whole system. Clarke specifies that Paul does not mean he was more exemplary in loving the pure law of God than his peers, but that he was more profoundly skilled in the traditions of the fathers, the accumulated oral interpretations beyond the written law. Wesley notes his zeal for the unwritten traditions specifically—those overlaid by rabbinic tradition on top of the written Torah. Barnes notes that alongside his superior advantages of education, Paul's characteristic ardor meant he outpaced his contemporaries in everything he applied himself to.

voices.html: Profited means made progress—cutting forward beyond his peers. The kind of progress corresponds to the full scope of Judaism: not merely theological knowledge but increase in zeal and strictness of ritualistic observance, mastery of tradition, and competitive religious achievement. All of this makes his conversion more, not less, remarkable.

Paul does not mean that he was more exemplary in the love and practice of the pure law of God than any of his countrymen, but that he was more profoundly skilled in the traditions of the fathers—the oral tradition over and above what was written in the law. This was the mark of Pharisaic distinction in his generation.

Paul surpassed others in his zeal for the Jewish religion and in defending its interests. He had better advantages than most of his countrymen, and by his great zeal and characteristic ardor he advanced beyond all his contemporaries. This past achievement makes his conversion all the more evidently a work of divine power.

15.synthesis:
The pivot in Paul's autobiography comes in verse 15: when God, who set me apart from my mother's womb and called me by his grace, was pleased to reveal his Son in me. Three claims compound here. First, the setting apart: God's purpose for Paul preceded his birth—this is election language drawn directly from Jeremiah 1:5 and Isaiah 49:1. Second, the call: it came not through Paul's merit but through grace, overriding and reversing his entire prior course. Third, the timing: when he was pleased—the moment was God's choice, not Paul's, and no human agent determined it.

Ellicott emphasizes that Paul lays stress on the predestination of his conversion and mission because the whole point is to show that what happened was throughout the work of God, not man. Clarke stresses the dual dimension—the God of nature who preserved him as a helpless infant and the God of grace who saved him as an adult persecutor. Wesley notes that Paul ascribes nothing to his own merits, endeavors, or sincerity; the unconditional predestination here may coexist with God's justice and mercy. Barnes sees Paul tracing all his hopes and all the good influences ever borne on his mind to God alone.

voices.html: In pursuance of his main argument, Paul lays stress upon the fact that his very conversion and mission had been first predestinated in the divine counsels and afterward carried out through divine interposition. It was throughout the work of God and not of man. No human agent set this chain of events in motion; the decision originated in eternity before Paul was born.

God preserved Paul by his providence when he was a helpless infant, and saved him by his grace when he was an adult persecutor. The same God who oversaw his nature from the womb also overruled his rebellion. This double work—natural and gracious—makes Paul's apostleship a testimony not to his own resources but to God's sovereign purpose.

Paul ascribes nothing to his own merits, endeavors, or sincerity. God set him apart as he did Jeremiah for a prophet. Such unconditional predestination as this may consist with both God's justice and mercy—the call overrides Paul's prior hostility, not because Paul deserved grace but precisely because grace is grace.

16.synthesis:
The purpose of the revelation was twofold: that God might reveal his Son in Paul (an interior transformation of knowing), and so that Paul might preach him among the Gentiles. The preposition in is significant. Ellicott takes it to mean in his mind or consciousness—the intense inward conviction that preceded and made possible all outward proclamation. Before Paul could preach Christ to others, he needed to know Christ himself, not merely know about him.

The immediate consequence is equally significant: I did not immediately consult with flesh and blood. Having received the revelation, Paul did not seek human validation or instruction. This is not a statement of independence for its own sake—it is evidence that the revelation was complete and sufficient. Calvin notes that the phrase to reveal his Son in me expresses the inner transformation that made external proclamation possible. Wesley adds that the inward revelation by the Spirit and the outward heavenly vision together equipped Paul for the mission, but he needed to know Christ before he could commend him to others. Clarke sees this as the appointment to preach to Gentiles specifically—the mission to the nations was embedded in the very act of conversion.

voices.html: The phrase reveal his Son in me expresses the design of Paul's apostleship—to make Christ known—and also the manner of it: an inward illumination of the soul that preceded and enabled all outward preaching. Without this interior revelation, there would be nothing to proclaim; with it, Paul needs no human supplement.

The revelation was in Paul's mind and consciousness. Before he could preach Christ to the Gentiles he needed first that intense inward conviction wrought in him during the mental struggle that followed his conversion. The knowledge was not theoretical but transformative—it changed who he was before it changed what he said.

God revealed his Son in Paul by the powerful operation of his Spirit, as well as to him by the heavenly vision. Paul did not confer with flesh and blood, being fully satisfied with the divine teaching. He who has received Christ by the Spirit's inward work has no need to seek human endorsement before beginning to proclaim him.

17.synthesis:
The negative geography of verse 17 is part of the argument: I did not go up to Jerusalem to those who were apostles before me, but I went away into Arabia, and returned again to Damascus. Each clause dismantles a possible claim that Paul was shaped by the Jerusalem circle. He did not go up to Jerusalem. He did not consult those who were apostles before him. Instead he withdrew to Arabia—a region outside the circle of apostolic influence—and then returned to the city where his conversion had taken place.

Calvin explains that this means he did not ask the authority of any man, not even of the apostles themselves. The mention of the apostles is not to diminish them but to show that Paul's gospel did not flow through them. Wesley simply notes the geographical sequence: Arabia first, then back to Damascus—and supposes his readers already know the Damascus road story. Clarke traces the route Paul had already made from Damascus to Jerusalem to the wider region. Barnes stresses that this was the apostle's way of saying he began his ministry immediately under divine instruction without waiting for ecclesiastical authorization from the founding church.

voices.html: Paul explains that he did not ask the authority of any man—not even of the apostles themselves. This does not diminish the apostles; it establishes that they were not the source of his gospel. His immediate retreat to Arabia and return to Damascus shows that his commission came directly and required no endorsement from those who had received theirs earlier.

The aim of the apostle is to show that he had his call so immediately and pointedly from God himself that he had no need of the concurrence even of the apostles. He was appointed by the same authority and fitted with the same gospel, and therefore had no need to derive instruction from those who had received their commission before him.

Paul did not go to Jerusalem at once. He did not consult with the apostles there or seek instruction from them about the nature of the Christian religion. The design of this statement is to show that he did not derive his commission from man. He went to Arabia before going anywhere near the men who might be supposed to have taught him.

18.synthesis:
Only after three years did Paul go up to Jerusalem—and even then his purpose was not to receive instruction but to visit Peter (ἱστορῆσαι Κηφᾶν—the word means to get to know, to become personally acquainted with). The fifteen-day stay was brief. He saw no other apostle, only James the Lord's brother. This carefully calibrated visit is the opposite of a student sitting at the feet of teachers: it is a peer exchange between men of equal standing, each commissioned independently.

Calvin emphasizes that Paul had already been exercising the apostolic office for three years before this visit, so he was not a novice seeking instruction. The purpose was fellowship and acquaintance, not formation. Ellicott notes the three years may be reckoned from the conversion itself and need not mean three complete years. Clarke stresses the verb ἱστορῆσαι—to become personally acquainted with, not to be taught by. Wesley reads the three years as the period in which Paul had already given full proof of his apostleship through his preaching—then he went to visit Peter.

voices.html: It was not till three years after Paul had begun to discharge the apostolic office that he went to Jerusalem. So he did not, at the outset, receive his calling from men. And when he did go, he went not to be instructed but to become acquainted with Peter—as a peer visits a peer, not as a student goes to a teacher.

The Greek word historēsai means to become personally acquainted with—not to receive instruction from. Paul visited Peter as a fellow apostle, not as a pupil. The brevity of the stay—fifteen days—and the limited company he kept confirm that he was not seeking a course of instruction from the Jerusalem church.

After three years, in which Paul had given full proof of his apostleship through preaching and suffering, he went to visit Peter—to converse with him as an equal, to strengthen the bond of fellowship between apostles who served in different spheres, not to receive from Peter anything that he lacked.

19.synthesis:
Of all the apostles Paul saw only one—James the Lord's brother. This James is distinguished from the son of Zebedee and from the son of Alphaeus by the title Lord's brother. Whether James was a full brother, a half-brother, or a cousin of Jesus was debated from early times, but all the commentators agree that he held apostolic rank and exercised governing authority in the Jerusalem church.

Calvin notes that Paul's sole purpose in the visit was a brief exchange with Peter and this one other figure—nothing more. He attended to no broader business in Jerusalem. Ellicott observes that the form of the Greek implies James was counted among the apostles, though the exact sense of his apostleship depends on who he was. Wesley identifies James as the Lord's kinsman and notes that in Acts 9:27, when Barnabas is said to have brought Paul to the apostles, only Peter and James are in fact meant. Barnes reviews the two Jameses in the apostolic lists and concludes that this is a distinct figure, the Lord's brother, who held authority in the Jerusalem church without being one of the original Twelve in the same sense.

voices.html: Paul's visit to Jerusalem had one object: a brief acquaintance with Peter and an encounter with James the Lord's brother. He attended to nothing else. This narrow scope of the visit is the very point: he could not have received his gospel from Jerusalem, since he barely touched the edges of its apostolic circle before departing again.

From the form of this phrase it appears that James the Lord's brother was considered to be an apostle. In what sense he held that office depends on identifying him—whether a full brother, a cousin, or something else. Whatever the precise relationship to Jesus, all agree he exercised governing authority in the Jerusalem church and was counted among the leading figures of the earliest Christian community.

That the James here referred to was an apostle is clear from the context. He is distinct from James the son of Zebedee and James the son of Alphaeus. He was the Lord's brother—whether by blood or by close kinship—and occupied a position of special authority in Jerusalem. Paul's brief encounter with him and Peter is the full extent of his contact with the apostolic college on this visit.

20.synthesis: Paul pauses his narrative to insert a solemn oath: Now in what I am writing to you, before God, I do not lie. The oath covers the whole account he has given of his limited contact with Jerusalem. Calvin notes that Paul's resort to an oath shows the vast earnestness of his argument: oaths are lawfully employed only on great and weighty occasions, and this is one. His opponents are accusing him of dependence on the Jerusalem apostles; he calls God as his witness that the account he has just given is strictly accurate. Clarke sees the oath as bearing specifically on the statement that he saw only Peter and James—proving beyond dispute that he had not received his commission from any apostolic assembly or council. Barnes observes that even in the midst of a theological argument, Paul does not hesitate to invoke divine sanction for his personal testimony, because the biographical facts are not incidental to the argument—they are the argument.

voices.html: Paul's affirmation by oath extends to the whole narrative. His resort to this solemn form shows how earnest he is about establishing the facts. Oaths cannot be employed lawfully except on great and weighty occasions; Paul uses one here because the truth of his independence from Jerusalem is the entire foundation of his defense of the gospel he preached.

This oath bears specifically on Paul's claim that he saw only Peter and James in Jerusalem—proving that he had not received his commission from the apostolic assembly, nor learned his gospel from them. Every claim he makes about his independence from human authority is underwritten by this solemn appeal to God as witness.

This is a solemn appeal to God. The design is to prevent all suspicion of falsehood. It may seem remarkable that Paul should make this appeal in a personal narrative, but the biographical facts are not incidental to his argument—they are the argument itself. Their accuracy is as important as any theological proposition he could assert.

21.synthesis:
After the Jerusalem visit Paul went into the regions of Syria and Cilicia—away from Judea, away from the apostolic center, into Gentile territory. Acts traces the route: from Jerusalem to Caesarea Maritima, by sea to Tarsus (Paul's home city in Cilicia), and eventually to Antioch in Syria. The argument continues: if he had been a pupil of Jerusalem, he would have stayed in Jerusalem or its environs. Instead he went to the places where his own mission lay.

Ellicott traces the route with reference to Acts, noting that Paul was first conveyed secretly to the sea-port Caesarea and then took ship for Syria and Cilicia. Clarke follows Acts 9:30 closely, connecting the escape from Jerusalem to the extended ministry in Gentile regions. Barnes notes that Paul omits from his account the controversy with the Hellenists in Acts 9:29 as not material to his present purpose, which is solely to establish his apostolic independence from the Jerusalem circle.

voices.html: The course Paul followed was this: he was first conveyed secretly to the sea-port Caesarea; there he took ship for the north; and his course lay through Syria and Cilicia, the last being his native province and Tarsus his home city. He departed from Jerusalem without lingering in Judea, heading instead toward the Gentile regions where his mission lay.

Paul's course after his conversion was: from Damascus to Jerusalem, and from Jerusalem into Syria and Cilicia. He was not commissioned or shaped by the Judean churches. The route took him away from the apostolic center and toward the regions of his Gentile mission, which is exactly what his argument requires.

Paul omits his controversy with the Hellenists in Jerusalem—that detail is not material to his present argument. His purpose here is simply to show that after the brief Jerusalem visit he went not deeper into Judea but outward into Gentile territory. He was not formed by the Jerusalem church; he departed from it as soon as he had paid his brief respects.

22.synthesis:
Paul's point sharpens: he was unknown by face to the churches of Judea. They had not seen him. The word remained (imperfect tense in the Greek) suggests continued unknowing—he did not pass through their assemblies or receive instruction from their elders. His only contact with the Judean Christian world was the brief Jerusalem visit just described. The Judean churches knew of him only by report.

Calvin notes that this fact is added to show even more strongly the wickedness of his slanderers: if the Judean churches—who had most reason to distrust Paul—were led to glorify God for him, how much less should those who know him only from slander condemn him? Ellicott stresses the imperfect aspect: he continued unknown, not merely failed to meet them on one occasion. Clarke notes that Paul knew only those at Jerusalem—he had not preached in any Christian congregation in Judea outside the capital. Barnes observes that from the outset Paul regarded himself as called to the Gentiles and did not remain even to form acquaintance with the Judean churches.

voices.html: That the churches of Judea glorified God for Paul, while not even knowing him by face, is an evident proof that his ministry was approved even among those who had the greatest reason to scrutinize it. If the churches nearest to the scene of his conversion praised God for him, how much less should distant critics who have only heard slanders condemn him?

The Greek is emphatic: I continued unknown. Not merely on one occasion did he fail to encounter them; throughout his time in Syria and Cilicia the Judean congregations had no personal knowledge of him. His independence from Jerusalem's apostolic circle was not occasional but sustained.

Paul had visited Jerusalem only, and he had formed no acquaintance with any of the churches in the other parts of Judea. He regarded himself from the first as called to preach particularly to the Gentiles and did not remain even to form a connection with the churches nearest to the original center of the faith.

23.synthesis:
The Judean churches knew Paul only by a remarkable report: the one who formerly persecuted us is now preaching the faith he once tried to destroy. The irony embedded in this report is Paul's final argument for divine origin: no human strategy produces this kind of result. A man who set out to destroy the faith does not end by proclaiming it unless something has intervened that is more powerful than his own will.

Ellicott notes that the faith here is not yet quite the body of Christian doctrine in its fully developed sense but the cardinal doctrine of faith in Christ—the one thing that defines the new community. Clarke observes that as a persecutor Paul was well known; as a convert he was no less known. The fame of both spread even where he was personally unseen. Barnes notes that this remarkable fact could hardly be concealed: the man who had been most active in destroying the church was now its most energetic advocate, and this report spread through the whole Judean church.

voices.html: The faith here is not quite the full body of Christian doctrine but the cardinal doctrine of faith in Christ—the one defining characteristic of the new community. The Judean churches had not seen Paul but heard of him: the man who destroyed this faith now preaches it. No report could better demonstrate that his conversion was genuine and his message divine.

As a persecutor of the Church, Paul was well known throughout Judea; as a convert to Christ he was no less so. The fame of both spread even where he was personally unseen. They had heard only—they had not seen him—and yet that heard report was enough to make them glorify God on his behalf.

The remarkable fact of Paul's conversion could hardly be concealed. He had been publicly prominent as a persecutor; his reversal was equally public. The Judean churches could not have seen him, but they had heard—and what they heard was the most convincing imaginable testimony that the power at work in him was not human.

24.synthesis:
Chapter 1 closes with a doxology from below: they glorified God in me. Not because of Paul, and certainly not Paul himself—but God, manifest in what he had done through Paul. The churches of Judea saw in this former persecutor turned preacher not a human achievement but a divine act, and their response was worship. This is the proper Christian response to gifted leaders: admiration passes through the person to its true object in God.

Ellicott captures it beautifully: an eminent Christian is like a city set on a hill, but the admiration that attaches to him does not rest in him—it becomes the occasion for giving praise to God. Calvin quotes Oecumenius: He does not say they praised me but they glorified God. Clarke notes that the churches gave God all the credit: they owed Paul's transformation to God's grace, not to Paul's virtue. Barnes summarizes: they regarded him as a true convert and sincere Christian, and praised God that such a persecutor had been made a preacher—proof that Paul owed his commission and his character to no human source.

voices.html: He does not say they praised or glorified Paul, but they glorified God. He says they glorified God in him—for all that belonged to Paul was from the grace of God. This is an evident proof that his ministry was approved by all the churches of Judea, and a decisive refutation of those who slandered him in Galatia.

This verse represents the proper attitude of Christian admiration. An eminent Christian is like a city set on a hill, but the admiration he attracts does not rest in him—it becomes the occasion for praising God. The Judean churches saw in Paul's transformation not a human story but a divine work, and they responded accordingly.

The Judean churches praised God that he had converted such a persecutor and made him a preacher of the gospel. The design for which Paul mentions this is to show that throughout his history—from his former persecution to his present preaching—his commission was from God, not from men. Those who knew the facts best glorified God for them.

1.synthesis:
Fourteen years after his conversion Paul went up to Jerusalem again, this time with Barnabas and taking Titus. The debate about which Jerusalem visit this corresponds to has occupied commentators for centuries. Calvin argues it cannot with certainty be identified with the Acts 15 council; examining the historical sequence, he concludes it more likely refers to an earlier visit. Ellicott and Wesley both identify it with Acts 15, seeing in the shared subject matter—the question of Gentile obligation to the law—the most natural correspondence.

What all parties agree on is the argument's purpose: Paul went by revelation, not by summons from Jerusalem or commission from a higher authority. The revelation demonstrates again that God is directing his apostle's movements, including his contacts with the Jerusalem church. Titus's presence—an uncircumcised Greek—is itself a test case for the question at issue. Barnes notes the chapter is closely connected with chapter 1 and should not have been divided from it; the argument about Paul's gospel and his independence from human authority continues without interruption.

voices.html: This cannot with certainty be affirmed to be the same journey mentioned in Acts 15. The connection of history leads rather to the opposite conclusion. Paul performed four journeys to Jerusalem; this appears to correspond to an earlier one. What is certain is that he went by revelation—God directed the journey—and this is what matters for the argument: even his contacts with Jerusalem were by divine appointment, not human obligation.

Fourteen years seems to be reckoned from the visit to Jerusalem just mentioned in chapter 1. Wesley and most interpreters identify this visit with the Jerusalem council of Acts 15, where the question of Gentile obligation to circumcision was formally settled. The shared subject matter—Titus's uncircumcision, the pressure from Judaizers—makes the identification compelling.

Fourteen years after my first journey to Jerusalem I went up again—this seems to be the journey mentioned in Acts 15, where several passages here refer to the great council in which all the apostles showed they were of the same judgment with Paul on the question of Gentile freedom from the ceremonial law.

Paul went up by revelation, not for the purpose of receiving instruction from the apostles there. It is to be remembered that his design throughout is to show that he had not received the gospel from men. He is careful, even in describing this Jerusalem conference, to note that God sent him there—he was not summoned by the church.

1.key_tension: Calvin identifies this visit with a journey earlier than the Jerusalem council of Acts 15, while Ellicott and Wesley identify it with that council, making the dating and historical placement of the episode a point of genuine interpretive division.

2.synthesis:
Paul laid before the Jerusalem leaders the gospel he preaches among the Gentiles—but he did so privately, before the prominent men. The private conference is not evasion; it is wisdom. A public confrontation with the Judaizers before the leaders had heard him would have produced confusion rather than clarity. By meeting the pillars first, Paul ensured that whatever the outcome of the assembly, the most authoritative voices would have heard and understood his position.

The phrase lest somehow I should be running or had run in vain is sometimes misread as doubt about his own gospel. Calvin clarifies: it means rather that if Paul's proclamation were to be publicly contradicted by the Jerusalem apostles without any prior meeting, the result would be confusion among the Gentile churches—not that Paul feared his gospel was wrong. Ellicott notes that the revelations guiding Paul often came in visions or trances. Wesley simply states that Paul laid before them the gospel touching justification by faith alone, not that they might confirm him therein, but that he might remove prejudice and prevent false brethren from using the apostles against him.

voices.html: Paul went up to Jerusalem not for instruction but to prove his apostleship and doctrine by a divine revelation. God directed that journey to confirm what he had already been teaching. The private conference with the leaders was not concealment but wisdom: it prevented the false brethren from using the apostolic authority against him before the facts could be heard.

Revelations were vouchsafed to Paul in various ways—in dreams, in nocturnal visions, in trance. Whatever the form here, the point is that God directed this visit. Paul laid before the Jerusalem leaders the gospel he preaches among the Gentiles, not to receive their verdict on it but to ensure that the most authoritative voices in the church had heard it from him directly.

Paul laid before them the gospel he preaches among the Gentiles—touching justification by faith alone—not that they might confirm him therein, but that he might prevent false brethren from using the apostles against his mission. The private meeting secured that the pillars of the Jerusalem church would not be uninformed weapons in his opponents' hands.

3.synthesis:
The evidence Paul offers for the Jerusalem leaders' endorsement of his gospel is negative but decisive: not even Titus, who was with me, was compelled to be circumcised, though he was a Greek. Titus was an uncircumcised Gentile believer—exactly the kind of person the Judaizers insisted must be circumcised. The apostles in Jerusalem did not require it. This is practical confirmation that the pillars agreed with Paul's position before any formal resolution was issued.

Calvin stresses that the apostles had brought an uncircumcised man and did not hesitate to acknowledge him as a brother—this is agreement in deed, not merely in word. Ellicott notes the parenthetical force of verses 3–6: Paul turns aside from his main narrative to give this one emphatic piece of evidence before returning to the outcome of the conference in verse 7. Wesley states that none of the apostles insisted on circumcising Gentile believers—Titus's case settles it. Barnes frames the Titus episode as a test case precisely in point: here was a man Paul had admitted to the Christian community without circumcision, and the Jerusalem apostles ratified that admission.

voices.html: The apostles acknowledged an uncircumcised man as a brother without hesitation. Paul had brought Titus to them as a living demonstration of his gospel's claims. Their failure to require his circumcision is not a minor procedural detail—it is the definitive act confirming that the Jerusalem leaders held the same views as Paul about Gentile freedom from the ceremonial law.

This and the two following verses are parenthetical. The result of the private conference is not given until verse 7, but without waiting for that, Paul turns aside to give one emphatic piece of evidence: Titus was not compelled to be circumcised. The apostles' handling of this living test case speaks louder than any formal resolution could.

Paul introduces the case of Titus to show that circumcision was not necessary to salvation. It was a case exactly in point: an uncircumcised Gentile believer brought to the Jerusalem leaders, and they did not require him to receive circumcision. This practical agreement preceded and undergirded whatever formal conclusions the conference reached.

4.synthesis:
Verse 4 is grammatically broken—Paul begins a clause about false brothers that he does not finish, his emotion carrying him forward before the syntax can catch up. What is clear is that the pressure to circumcise Titus came not from the apostles but from false brothers who had been secretly brought in to spy out our freedom which we have in Christ Jesus, so that they might bring us into bondage. The false brothers are infiltrators: they came in by stealth, their purpose surveillance rather than fellowship.

Ellicott notes that the apostolic leaders resisted the pressure of these agitators, which is why Titus was not compelled. Calvin explains that Paul may have been aware the false brothers would use any circumcision as ammunition—so he refused to give them any. Wesley identifies them as those who urged circumcision and slipped into private conferences, seeking to bring Gentile believers back under the yoke of the ceremonial law. Barnes believes these were Judaizing Christians—people who claimed to be Christians and who insisted that Gentile converts must also observe the law of Moses.

voices.html: The apostolic leaders resisted the pressure of these agitators—which is why Titus was ultimately not compelled. The false brothers had come in stealthily, their purpose being surveillance: to find grounds for claiming that Paul's Gentile converts were not truly in the covenant unless they were circumcised and law-observant.

False brothers had slipped into the private conferences at Jerusalem to spy out the liberty from the ceremonial law that Gentile believers enjoyed—so that, if possible, they might bring those believers back into the bondage of the law. Their presence was not fellowship but infiltration, their goal not understanding but entrapment.

These false brothers were probably Judaizing Christians—people who claimed to follow Christ while insisting that Gentile converts must also observe the law of Moses. They came not to learn but to discover what Paul was teaching and to find grounds for requiring circumcision of his Gentile converts and undermining the freedom of the gospel.

5.synthesis:
To them we did not yield in submission even for a moment, so that the truth of the gospel might remain with you. Paul's resistance was total and principled. He did not make temporary concessions for the sake of peace; he did not negotiate a middle ground. The reason he gives for this inflexibility is pastoral rather than merely polemical: the truth of the gospel must remain with the Galatians. Any ground yielded to the false brothers in Jerusalem would have implications for every Gentile believer.

Calvin calls this steadiness the seal of Paul's doctrine: when the false brothers exerted their utmost and Paul stood firm, no room for doubt remained. Wesley notes the remarkable discrimination Paul showed: he circumcised Timothy voluntarily for the sake of weak brothers who might be offended, but he refused to circumcise Titus under pressure from false brothers who were trying to compel it. The distinction is between love-motivated accommodation and principle-violating capitulation. Clarke stresses that Paul was so fully satisfied with his divine call that he did not submit in the least to the opinion of Judaizing teachers.

voices.html: This steadiness was the seal of Paul's doctrine. When false brothers who wished for nothing more than a ground of accusation exerted themselves to the utmost, and Paul stood firm, there could no longer be any room for doubt. His refusal to yield was not stubbornness but faithfulness: the truth of the gospel was at stake, and he would not trade it for peace.

Paul was so fully satisfied with his divine call, and that he had acted in strict conformity to it, that he did not submit in the least to the opinion of Judaizing teachers. To give way even for an hour would have implied that their position had merit—and their position, if granted, would have destroyed the liberty of every Gentile believer.

With wonderful prudence Paul used his Christian liberty: circumcising Timothy because of weak brothers who needed accommodation, but refusing to circumcise Titus because false brothers were trying to compel it. The difference is decisive—love bends toward the weak; faithfulness resists those who would make law-keeping a condition of salvation.

6.synthesis:
Paul resumes his account of the Jerusalem conference: those who were reputed to be something—whatever they were makes no difference to him, for God shows no partiality—added nothing to his gospel. The phrasing is deliberately ambivalent. Paul is not disparaging the Jerusalem apostles; he is insisting that their personal prestige does not bear on the question of whether his gospel is true. The gospel is not more true because Peter approves it, nor less true if Peter had questioned it.

Calvin explains that Paul uses the phrase those who seemed to be somewhat in its honorific sense—men of the highest reputation—without intending to diminish them. The point is simply that God's impartiality means human eminence does not make the truth more true. Ellicott notes that Paul returns here from his parenthesis about Titus to continue the main narrative: the outcome of the conference was that the pillars added nothing to his gospel, confirming that what he had been preaching was already complete and correct. Wesley states it plainly: whatever their eminence in gifts or outward prerogatives, God accepts no man's person—and they in conference added nothing to Paul's doctrine.

voices.html: The men of highest reputation added nothing to Paul's gospel. He uses the phrase not to belittle them—they were indeed of high character—but to insist that personal prestige does not determine the truth. God shows no partiality, which means that the eminence of Peter or James does not make the gospel more true or false than it already is.

Paul returns from his parenthesis to give the result of the private conference: the pillars added nothing to his message. This is the decisive outcome. They had heard his gospel, they had examined it, and they had nothing to add—which means that what Paul had been preaching independently was already complete and did not require apostolic supplementation.

However great the apostles were—and they were undoubtedly something above all others—God accepts no man's person for eminence in gifts or outward prerogatives. In their conference they added nothing to Paul's doctrine. What he had received by revelation was already complete; the Jerusalem leaders confirmed it rather than constituting it.

7.synthesis:
The positive outcome of the conference: the pillars saw that Paul had been entrusted with the gospel of the uncircumcision, just as Peter had been entrusted with the gospel of the circumcision. This is not two gospels but one gospel preached to two different audiences. The recognition is crucial: they saw it. The evidence was before them in the form of Paul's ministry, the converts he had made, the character of his preaching—and they recognized what they saw as the same gospel they preached, manifested in a different sphere.

Calvin notes that the apostles gave their testimony to his doctrine immediately and without exception—they produced nothing on the other side but acknowledged that he held the same gospel as themselves. Ellicott notes that the elder apostles recognized Paul because his teaching was fundamentally the same as their own, even though expressed to a different audience. Wesley traces the recognition to the effects Paul laid before them—the visible fruit of his Gentile mission was the proof. Barnes observes that Paul had received this specific commission to the Gentiles at his conversion, and the Jerusalem leaders now formally acknowledged it.

voices.html: The apostles gave their testimony to Paul's doctrine without exception. They produced nothing on the other side, as is commonly done in disputed matters, but acknowledged that he held the same gospel as themselves. This is the most striking confirmation possible: the men who had been with Jesus from the beginning recognized his gospel as the same as theirs.

The elder apostles recognized Paul because they saw that his teaching was fundamentally the same as their own. The gospel of the uncircumcision means a gospel for the uncircumcised—not a different message but the same gospel entrusted to a different apostle for a different audience. The success of Paul among Gentiles proved that his commission was as divine as Peter's commission to the Jews.

Paul had received the specific duty of preaching the gospel to the Gentiles when he was converted and called to the ministry. The Jerusalem leaders now perceived this—they saw the evidence of it in the fruit of his mission—and they acknowledged that God had entrusted the Gentile world to Paul as he had entrusted the Jewish world to Peter.

8.synthesis:
The ground for the recognition in verse 7 is stated here: for he who worked through Peter for his apostleship to the circumcised worked also through me for mine to the Gentiles. The same God who equipped and empowered Peter's Jewish mission equipped and empowered Paul's Gentile mission. The evidence of God's working is the evidence of equal divine authorization. The Galatians know what Paul's ministry produced; the Jerusalem leaders could see it laid before them.

Ellicott notes with characteristic precision that worked effectually and was mighty in the English translation represent exactly the same Greek verb—an inconsistency that obscures the parallel Paul is drawing. The same divine energy was at work in both apostles. Clarke states it simply: God gave them both the talents suited to their respective callings and gave each equal success in their different departments. Wesley notes that the same God qualified each apostle for and sustained each in his office—Peter's toward Jews, Paul's toward Gentiles.

voices.html: Worked effectually and was mighty are the same Greek word—our translators have unnecessarily used two different phrases. The same divine energy that equipped Peter for the Jewish mission equipped Paul for the Gentile mission. This is the argument: because the same God worked through both with equal power, both commissions carry equal authority.

God who worked powerfully with Peter, worked powerfully also with Paul. He gave both the talents suited to their work and equal success in their different departments. The Jerusalem apostles could see this—the evidence of God's working in Paul's ministry was the basis on which they recognized his commission as divine.

The same God who qualified Peter for and supported him in the discharge of his apostleship to the Jews worked likewise in Paul for and in the discharge of his office toward the Gentiles. Equal divine energy in two spheres means equal divine authorization—which is exactly what the Jerusalem pillars acknowledged when they extended the right hand of fellowship.

9.synthesis:
James, Cephas, and John—recognized as pillars—gave Paul and Barnabas the right hand of fellowship. The gesture is formal and binding: an acknowledgment of partnership in a shared mission, a recognition of equal standing between the Jerusalem circle and the Gentile mission. The division of labor is made explicit: we to the Gentiles and they to the circumcised. This is not segregation but specialization—the same gospel carried to different peoples by different authorized messengers.

Ellicott observes that the order James, Cephas, John rather than the expected Peter-first order is significant: in Jerusalem, James held the governing position. Calvin notes that those who contempt the grace which moved the most eminent apostles to admire Paul are charged with hateful and proud disdain. Wesley identifies James as bishop of the Jerusalem church and notes that John's presence at the council, though not mentioned in Acts explicitly, is confirmed by this verse. Clarke explains that the term pillars denoted men of the highest acknowledged eminence in the community.

voices.html: Those who treat with contempt the grace of God—by which even the most eminent apostles were led to acknowledge Paul—are charged with hateful and proud disdain. The pillars of Jerusalem extended the right hand of fellowship: this is the most authoritative possible endorsement of Paul's gospel and mission, and those who ignore it reveal only their own arrogance.

James is named first here—not Peter—because in Jerusalem James held the governing position. The right hand of fellowship is a formal gesture acknowledging partnership. The division of spheres is practical, not doctrinal: the same gospel will flow to Jews through one set of messengers and to Gentiles through another, but the message is one.

James is named first because he was bishop of the church in Jerusalem. John's presence at the council, though not explicitly mentioned in Acts, is confirmed here. These who were undoubtedly pillars gave the right hand of fellowship—an acknowledgment that Paul and Barnabas carried the same gospel commission for the Gentile world that the Twelve carried for the Jewish world.

10.synthesis:
The one practical request the Jerusalem pillars added was that Paul and Barnabas should remember the poor—specifically, the poor among the Judean believers who had suffered impoverishment for their faith. Paul adds immediately: the very thing I was eager to do. This single request is notable for what it is not: it is not a doctrinal supplement, not a requirement about circumcision, not a modification of Paul's gospel. The only thing Jerusalem asked of Paul was an act of charity.

Calvin notes that the Judean believers labored under extreme poverty, arising from the calamities that befell the whole nation and from the cruel persecution they suffered from their countrymen. Ellicott observes that Paul had already been the means of bringing contributions from the Antioch church to Jerusalem (Acts 11:29-30), so this was not a new demand but a continuation of an existing practice. Clarke notes that the apostles saw God had expressly called Paul as he had called them, and they attempted to give him no new injunctions except this one act of material solidarity. Wesley simply identifies the poor as the Christians in Judea who had lost everything for Christ's sake.

voices.html: It is evident that the brethren in Judea labored under extreme poverty—arising from the various calamities which befell the whole nation and from the cruel rage of their adversaries. The one request the pillars made was not doctrinal but practical: remember those who suffer for the faith. Paul agreed immediately, having already been doing exactly this.

The poor in Jerusalem had already received contributions from the Antioch church through Paul (Acts 11:29-30). This request was gracefully made, not only as an act of charity but as a recognition that the Gentile churches owed a spiritual debt to the Jewish Christian community from which the gospel had come. Paul was more than willing to honor that bond.

The Jerusalem leaders made one request: remember the poor Christians in Judea. This is the entire scope of what they asked Paul to add to his practice. No doctrinal supplement, no ceremonial requirement, no modification of his gospel—only an act of material solidarity with suffering believers. The very modesty of the request confirms how fully they accepted his commission and his message.

11.synthesis:
The confrontation at Antioch is the climax of Paul's biographical argument. Peter came to Antioch—the greatest Gentile church, the center of the Gentile mission—and Paul opposed him to his face, because Peter stood condemned by his own actions. The charge is not that Peter denied the gospel in words but that he enacted a denial of it in conduct: he withdrew from table fellowship with Gentile believers when men came from James, fearing those of the circumcision.

The disagreement about chronology matters: Calvin argues this confrontation preceded the Acts 15 council, which means Peter was acting without the benefit of the formal apostolic ruling. Ellicott places it after Acts 15:35, which means Peter acted inconsistently despite knowing the council's decision. Either reading makes Peter's conduct culpable, but the nature of the culpability differs: ignorance before the council, hypocrisy after it. Wesley notes that so far was Paul from receiving his doctrine from the chief apostles, or being inferior to them, that he publicly rebuked the most prominent among them to his face when truth required it. Barnes draws the same conclusion: Paul regarded himself as on a level with the chief apostles and acknowledged no inferiority.

voices.html: Whoever carefully examines all the circumstances will conclude that this confrontation happened before the Acts 15 council had ruled that Gentiles need not observe the ceremonial law. For Peter was drawing Gentile believers back toward legal observance—a decision he would not have made, one hopes, had the formal ruling already been issued. Calvin dates the episode early, making Peter's error one of indecision rather than hypocrisy.

The visit alluded to probably took place soon after the return of Paul and Barnabas in the interval described in Acts 15:35—after the council, not before it. Peter was therefore acting inconsistently with the known resolution of the Jerusalem church. His withdrawal from Gentile table fellowship was not ignorance but a failure of nerve in the face of social pressure from the circumcision party.

So far was Paul from receiving his doctrine from the chief apostles, or from being inferior to them, that he withstood Peter himself to the face. When truth demanded it, Paul stood alone against the most prominent figure in the apostolic circle. His willingness to do so is itself evidence that his authority came from a source above the apostolic hierarchy.

The design of this statement is evident: it shows that Paul regarded himself as on a level with the chief apostles and that he did not acknowledge his inferiority to any of them. He opposed Peter face to face—the most prominent of the Twelve—because principle required it. Such an act is only possible for a man who knows his commission is equal to the one he is correcting.

11.key_tension: Calvin argues the Antioch confrontation preceded the Jerusalem council of Acts 15, making Peter's error one of ignorance; Ellicott places it afterward, making it an act of inconsistency despite knowing the council's ruling—a significant difference in how Peter's culpability is understood.

12.synthesis:
The specific action that provoked the confrontation: before certain men came from James, Peter ate with the Gentiles; but when they arrived he drew back and separated himself, fearing those of the circumcision party. Peter had known and lived the truth—God had broken down the partition wall, and Peter himself had eaten with the Gentile Cornelius (Acts 10-11). He had acted on that truth freely until social pressure reversed him. Fear of what the circumcision party would think overrode what he knew to be right.

Clarke notes that Peter had been convinced that God pulled down the middle wall of partition and he had acted on this conviction; then, out of fear, he abandoned the practice he knew to be correct. Ellicott leaves open whether the men from James carried official authorization or simply came from the Jerusalem church; their presence was enough to intimidate. Barnes observes that these were Jewish converts who observed the Mosaic rites most strictly, and their arrival changed Peter's conduct completely—he who had eaten freely with Gentiles now avoided them.

voices.html: Whether the men from James brought official authorization or simply came from the Jerusalem church, their presence was enough. Peter, who had been eating freely with Gentile believers, suddenly drew back and separated himself—fearing the circumcision party's reaction. His action was not a return to conscientious scruple but a capitulation to social and political pressure.

Peter was convinced that God had pulled down the wall of partition between Jews and Gentiles—he had acted on this conviction, associating with Gentile believers and eating with them freely. Then, out of fear of those of the circumcision, he reversed course. He separated himself from those with whom he knew he ought to fellowship.

Peter had been eating with the Gentile believers. When men arrived who had been intimate with James and who observed the Jewish rites most strictly, Peter drew back and avoided the Gentiles—apparently in fear that his conduct would be reported unfavorably in Jerusalem. His reversal was complete and its effect on others in the church was immediate and damaging.

13.synthesis:
Peter's withdrawal was not a private inconsistency; it became a public epidemic. The other Jewish Christians at Antioch joined his dissimulation, and even Barnabas—Paul's closest companion in the Gentile mission—was carried away with it. The word for dissimulation (ὑπόκρισις) is hypocrisy in the literal Greek sense: acting a part, suppressing one's real convictions and enacting a different set. The Jewish believers at Antioch knew that the partition between Jew and Gentile had been abolished in Christ; they acted as though it had not been.

Ellicott notes that the other Jewish converts suppressed their real convictions and acted as though their convictions were different from what they were. Clarke observes that seeing Peter act this part, and also fearing the circumcision party, they separated themselves from Gentile believers—modeling exactly the behavior that would destroy the unity of the church. Barnes suggests the other Jews were probably led by Peter's example, as they would naturally regard him as a leader; his authority made his inconsistency contagious. Paul singles out Barnabas specifically because Barnabas was the last person anyone would have expected to yield: his involvement shows how sweeping and dangerous the collapse was.

voices.html: The dissimulation consisted in suppressing real convictions and acting as if from a different set of beliefs. The other Jewish believers at Antioch knew the partition wall had been abolished; they acted as if it had not. Even Barnabas—the companion of Paul's Gentile mission—was swept away. The contagion spread from Peter to the entire Jewish-Christian community at Antioch.

Seeing Peter act this part, and fearing those who were of the circumcision, the other Jewish converts separated themselves from Gentile believers—modeling a division that denied the unity Christ had created. Barnabas was drawn in too: the man who had stood with Paul against Judaizing pressure was borne away by the torrent of Peter's example.

The other Jews at Antioch were probably induced to dissemble by Peter's example—they would naturally regard him as a leader, and his withdrawal gave their own retreat the cover of apostolic precedent. Barnabas's involvement is the most troubling detail: his capitulation shows how thoroughly Peter's inconsistency had destabilized the community Paul had built.

14.synthesis:
Paul confronted Peter publicly: before them all. The rebuke had to be public because the sin was public; a private correction would not have undone the public damage Peter's withdrawal had caused. The charge is precise: if you, though a Jew, live like a Gentile and not like a Jew, how can you compel the Gentiles to live like Jews? Peter by his withdrawal was not merely failing to keep the law himself—he was creating social pressure that effectively compelled Gentile believers to Judaize if they wanted to maintain fellowship with the Jewish believers.

Ellicott notes that the Greek word for walk uprightly means to walk on straight feet, erect and straightforwardly—as opposed to shuffling or evading. Wesley captures the power of the scene: Paul stood alone against Peter and all the Jewish believers. Calvin explains the proper road: to unite Gentiles with Jews in such a manner that neither should be compelled to change their way of life. Barnes notes that their conduct was not honest—the word for walk not uprightly carries the sense of moral dishonesty, of not being what one claims to be.

voices.html: The proper road of the gospel was to unite Gentiles and Jews in such a manner that neither would be compelled to abandon their natural way of life in order to share fellowship. Peter's withdrawal created a different pressure: it compelled Gentiles to take up Jewish customs if they wanted to remain in communion. His action contradicted the liberty of the gospel while he himself lived as though the law had been abolished.

The Greek word for walking not uprightly means to walk on straight feet—erect and straightforward—as opposed to shuffling and evading. Peter was not living his convictions but enacting a different set. The public rebuke was necessary because the public conduct had damaged the community: the correction had to be as visible as the error it addressed.

Paul said to Cephas before them all: if you, being a Jew, live in your ordinary life after the manner of the Gentiles—not observing the ceremonial law, which you know to be now abolished—why do you compel the Gentiles to live as Jews? See Paul standing alone against Peter and all the Jewish believers. This is what gospel faithfulness looks like when authority and truth come into conflict.

15.synthesis:
Verse 15 begins what scholars have long debated: is this still Paul's speech to Peter, or has it become the letter's theological argument addressed to the Galatians? The content points toward the latter: Paul is drawing the universal principle that underlies the specific confrontation. We ourselves are Jews by nature and not Gentile sinners—the irony is sharp. Paul uses the Jewish perspective on Gentile moral standing (they are sinners, outside the covenant) not to endorse it but to set up the argument: even we, who have every natural advantage, know that justification does not come through the law.

Calvin notes that the phrasing may be an anticipation of an objection—what might be said on the Jewish side—rather than Paul's own position on Gentile moral status. Clarke explains that sinners of the Gentiles was a Jewish idiom meaning those without knowledge of the true God. Wesley specifies that Paul shifts here from addressing Peter in the second person to using we to include himself—a subtle grammatical signal that the argument is expanding beyond the immediate confrontation. Barnes notes the longstanding debate about where the speech to Peter ends and the epistle's argument begins.

voices.html: Some read this as an anticipation of what might be urged from the Jewish side, not as Paul's own assertion that Gentiles are morally inferior. In either reading, the point is the same: even those who have the highest natural religious advantages—birth into the covenant, the law, the traditions—know that before God justification does not come through any of these.

Sinners of the Gentiles was a standard Jewish idiom for those without knowledge of the true God—moral outsiders by Jewish reckoning. Paul uses the phrase from the Jewish perspective without endorsing its full implications. Even we who are Jews by nature and not sinners in this sense—even we know that the law cannot justify. How much more clearly does that apply to Gentile believers?

Paul speaks in the plural here to spare Peter—softening the personal confrontation by including himself in the argument. Jews by birth, not merely converts or proselytes, have natural advantages that Gentiles lack; yet even these advantages do not secure justification. The argument moves from the particular confrontation at Antioch to the universal principle that governs both Jewish and Gentile standing before God.

16.synthesis:
Verse 16 is the first explicit statement of the doctrine of justification in Galatians, and its triply repeated structure drives the point home with accumulating force. A person is not justified by works of the law but through faith in Jesus Christ—stated as common ground between Paul and Peter. We also have believed in Christ Jesus, in order to be justified by faith in Christ and not by works of the law—stated as their shared practice. Because by works of the law no one will be justified—stated as a scriptural absolute (citing Psalm 143:2), cutting off any remaining escape route.

Ellicott notes that this is the first appearance in the epistle of justify (δικαιόω), a word that will dominate the argument through chapter 3—meaning to be pronounced righteous, free from guilt and therefore from punishment, before God. Calvin insists that Paul is not merely saying works are insufficient without faith; he is saying works do not justify at all—not even partially. Wesley extends the scope: not even the moral law, much less the ceremonial. Clarke refers the reader to his extensive discussion in Romans 3, where the same doctrine is treated at length. Barnes stresses that the Jewish Christians Paul addresses had themselves come to know this truth—it was their own experience that law-keeping could not produce right standing before God.

voices.html: Paul does not merely state that ceremonies or works of any kind are insufficient without the assistance of faith. He meets the question with a statement admitting of no exception: not by works, but by the gift of Christ alone. To say that works contribute anything to justification is to make Christ's work partial—and a partial Christ cannot save.

Here for the first time in the epistle Paul introduces the word justify—to be pronounced righteous, free from guilt and therefore from punishment, in the sight of God. This condition cannot be produced by works done in obedience to the law because the law, by its nature, can only condemn those who fall short of its demands. Faith in Christ is the only basis on which God pronounces the sinner righteous.

A person is not justified by works of the law—not even of the moral law, much less the ceremonial. The law in its highest form cannot produce right standing before God because it cannot atone for past transgression and cannot supply the righteousness God requires. Only faith in Jesus Christ—trust in what he has done and who he is—brings the justification the law was never designed to give.

The Jewish Christians Paul addresses had themselves come to know that justification does not come through legal obedience—their own conversion was the evidence. We who are Jews by birth, with every religious advantage, found that we had to abandon the law-system for Christ. That experience, shared by all Jewish believers, is the experiential confirmation of the doctrinal point Paul is making.

17.synthesis:
An anticipated objection: if we Jewish believers sought justification in Christ and thereby abandoned the law, and if we are still found to be sinners (because the law finds everyone guilty), does that mean Christ has made us sinners—that Christ is a minister of sin? Paul's answer is emphatic: Certainly not! The objection confuses two different diagnoses of sin. The law's verdict that we are sinners does not make Christ the cause of our sin; it reveals our pre-existing condition and drives us to Christ who alone can deal with it.

Calvin notes that Paul here returns from the specific confrontation with Peter to address the Galatians directly. The logic is subtle: if justification requires going back to the law after abandoning it for Christ, then our initial abandonment of the law was itself sinful—which would mean Christ led us into sin. But that conclusion is absurd. Wesley states it simply: if we continue in sin, it does not follow that Christ countenances it. Clarke explains that those who submit to circumcision after trusting in Christ make themselves sinners against the very gospel they professed, not against the law—Christ is not responsible for that choice.

voices.html: If the law, in its verdict, finds even justified believers to be sinners, does Christ bear responsibility for their condition? Certainly not. Paul now addresses the Galatians directly rather than Peter. The objection rests on a confusion: the law's exposure of sin is not Christ's creation of sin. Christ did not make us sinners; he found us sinners and offers the only remedy the law cannot provide.

If, while we seek to be justified by faith in Christ, we ourselves are found sinners—submitting to circumcision, acknowledging the necessity of the law—we make ourselves transgressors. Not transgressors against the law, which we know to be superseded, but against the very gospel we professed. The fault is ours, not Christ's; he is not the minister of our inconsistency.

If the doctrine that justification comes by faith rather than law is pressed to the conclusion that Christ promotes sin—by removing the law's restraint—then that conclusion must be rejected. Christ does not promote sin by freeing believers from legal condemnation; he deals with sin at its root. The gospel is not a license; it is a new power and a new standing that the law could never supply.

18.synthesis:
Verse 18 presses the logic: if I rebuild what I tore down, I prove myself to be a transgressor. The first-person form is rhetorical—Paul speaks of himself but means anyone who does what Peter was in effect doing at Antioch. The Antioch withdrawal was precisely a rebuilding of the partition wall that Christ tore down. To require Gentile believers to separate at table from Jewish believers, or to require circumcision for full fellowship, is to reconstruct the law-system as though Christ had never abolished it—and that reconstruction is the transgression.

Calvin explains that this is the first part of Paul's reply to the objection in verse 17: the man who rebuilds the partition shows that he is the transgressor, not Christ. Ellicott sharpens it: the sin lies not in leaving the law for Christ but in going back from Christ to the law. Clarke states plainly that if Paul were to act like a Jew again, enjoining law-observance on Gentiles after having proved repeatedly that the law is abolished in Christ, he would build up what he destroyed and make himself—not Christ—the transgressor. Wesley notes that the objection in verse 17 would only have force if the gospel promised justification to men continuing in sin; it does not.

voices.html: This is Paul's first reply to the suggestion that seeking justification in Christ makes Christ a minister of sin. The refusal of the suggestion: the transgressor is not Christ but the one who tears down the law-system in principle and then rebuilds it in practice. Peter was rebuilding at Antioch what the gospel had torn down—and that act of rebuilding is the sin, not the initial tearing down.

The sin lies not in leaving the law for Christ but in going back from Christ to the law. The logic is doubled: on the theory that the law remains valid, it was wrong to have abandoned it; on the theory that Christianity supersedes it, going back is a betrayal of Christ. Either way, the transgression is in the rebuilding, not in the original demolition.

Paul uses the first person here but intends a general proposition: if anyone, having removed or destroyed what was evil, again introduces and establishes it, he does wrong. Applied to Peter at Antioch: by withdrawing from Gentiles, Peter was effectively rebuilding the very wall of separation that Christ demolished. The transgression is Peter's—not Christ's, and not Paul's gospel's.

19.synthesis:
Paul now speaks in his own person, not the rhetorical first-person of verse 18: For through the law I died to the law, so that I might live to God. The law itself—in its proper function of exposing sin and pronouncing condemnation—kills the hope that law-keeping can save. It is the law's own work that drives the sinner beyond the law to Christ. There is no tension between Paul's death to the law and his living to God; the death to the law is the precondition for the life toward God that the law itself always pointed to but could never produce.

Calvin explains that we must not ascribe to Christ the slaying of the law's disciples; that is the law's own work. The law does what it was designed to do—expose sin—and in doing so it creates the conditions for the gospel. Ellicott observes that the Apostle's thought shifts here from the general rhetorical use of I to his own personal experience. Clarke reads the verse as meaning: properly considering the nature of the law's requirements, Paul despaired of finding salvation there and took refuge in the gospel. Wesley: the Spirit applied the law to Paul's heart, deeply convicting him of his sinfulness—and that conviction is what freed him from the law as a ground of justification so that he might live to God as his true end.

voices.html: The law itself slays its disciples. It was not necessary for Christ to destroy the righteousness of the law—the law destroys hope in itself by exposing how completely we fail its demands. We must not ascribe to Christ what properly belongs to the law. Christ does not kill the law's adherents; the law does that work, and in doing so drives them beyond itself to the only righteousness that can satisfy God.

In the last verse Paul had been using a rhetorical first person; here he shifts to his own personal experience. The law drove him—through its uncompromising demands and its verdict of condemnation—to abandon it as a ground of justification. Death to the law is not antinomianism; it is the necessary passage from law-striving to Christ-trusting, from the dead end of self-achievement to the living source of divine righteousness.

Through the law—applied by the Spirit to Paul's heart, and deeply convicting him of his utter sinfulness and helplessness—he died to the law: to all hope of justification from it. For this very end he is freed from the law as a ground of standing before God, so that he might be freed from sin and live unto God as the one toward whom all true life is directed.

20.synthesis:
Galatians 2:20 is perhaps the most personally intense theological statement in all of Paul's letters. Three paradoxes compound in two verses: I have been crucified with Christ—yet I live. It is no longer I who live—yet Christ lives in me. The life I live in the flesh I live by faith—faith in the one who loved me and gave himself for me.

The first paradox: crucifixion with Christ means that the self which sought justification through law-keeping—the self constituted by its own effort and merit—was put to death when Christ died. Paul died with him. Yet Paul is alive. The second paradox resolves the first: the life that continues after crucifixion is not Paul's autonomous life but Christ's life in Paul. The identity is not dissolved but transformed from the inside out—Christ becomes the animating center rather than the ego.

The third element is the most tender: the Son of God, who loved me and gave himself for me. Paul shifts from the plural of the preceding argument to the singular me. The cosmic transaction—God's Son dying for the world—is not lost in abstraction; it was personal, directed at Paul specifically, at every believer specifically. Calvin traces the secret energy of the ingrafted branch drawing life from the root. Ellicott notes that the release from the ceremonial burden was only the beginning; what the law could never give—a living union with Christ that transforms the one who receives it—is what faith in Christ produces. Clarke reads the crucifixion as death to all expectation of justification by law, complete as Christ's own death on the cross. Wesley sees Christ as a fountain of life in the inmost soul, from which a spiritual resurrection is always proceeding. Barnes notes that Paul is not speaking of literal inactivity but of death to one thing—the law's claim on him as a ground of standing—while being fully alive to another: God, through Christ who lives and reigns in him.

voices.html: Being ingrafted into the death of Christ, Paul derives from it a secret energy, as the twig does from the root. The handwriting of the law which was contrary to us, Christ has nailed to his cross. Because Paul is made partaker of the death of Christ, the energy of the cross lives in him—not as memory but as present transforming power flowing from the risen, living Christ who now dwells within.

Release from the ceremonial burden was only the beginning; what the law could never give—a living union with Christ that transforms the one who receives it—is what faith produces. The life Paul now lives is not his own autonomous existence but the life of Christ expressed through his person. The ego is not destroyed but displaced from the center, replaced by the one who loved him and gave himself for him.
