Paul is as truly dead to all expectation of justification by law as Christ was dead when he gave up the ghost on the cross. Through Christ alone he lives—enjoys a spiritual life, is accepted of God, and looks for eternal glory. The crucifixion image is not ornamental: it marks the completeness of the death to self-achievement and the corresponding completeness of the new life in Christ.

The body of sin is destroyed when Paul is crucified with Christ. Christ is a fountain of life in his inmost soul, from which a spiritual resurrection is always proceeding. The faith by which Paul lives is not mere intellectual assent but a living trust directed at a specific person: the Son of God who loved him individually and gave himself for him personally.

In respect to one thing Paul was dead—the law's claim on him as a ground of justification. In respect to everything else he was fully alive. He is not inactive or insensible; he lives in the body, engaging the full range of human experience. But the animating principle of that life is no longer the ego striving toward the law's demands—it is faith in the one who, by his death, secured what Paul's effort could never achieve.

21.synthesis:
Chapter 2 closes with a reductio ad absurdum: I do not nullify the grace of God, for if righteousness were through the law, then Christ died for nothing. The Judaizing position, followed to its logical end, empties the cross of meaning. If law-keeping can produce right standing before God, then the death of the Son of God was an unnecessary event—a tragic mistake, or at best a piece of religious theater. The cross was not needed. Christ died in vain.

Calvin notes the great emphasis in this expression: how dreadful is the ingratitude of despising so invaluable a grace, obtained at so great a price. Yet this is precisely what the false apostles were doing—not by denying the cross but by supplementing it with legal requirements, which amounts to the same thing. Ellicott observes that the person who attaches himself to Christ rather than the law escapes the charge of thwarting God's free gift; it is the person who returns to the law who nullifies grace. Clarke states it simply: if law-observance could justify, then Christ's death was unnecessary—which Paul treats as an obvious impossibility rather than a theoretical position. Wesley: those who seek justification by the law make void the grace of God in Christ Jesus, regardless of their intentions; the logic of their position demolishes the cross.

voices.html: There is great emphasis here: how dreadful the ingratitude of despising grace so invaluable in itself and obtained at such a price. This heinous charge falls on those who are not satisfied with Christ alone but introduce legal requirements alongside him. To supplement the cross is to declare it insufficient—which is to nullify what it accomplished and to imply that the Son of God died for nothing.

The person who attaches himself to Christ rather than returning to the law escapes the charge of thwarting God's free gift. On the other hand, the person who goes back to the law for justification implies that the grace offered in Christ was not enough—that God's gift required human supplement. That position, followed consistently, concludes that Christ's death served no essential purpose.

If justification and salvation come by observance of the law, then Christ is dead in vain. Paul treats this conclusion not as a possibility to be refuted but as an absurdity that refutes the premise. No one who has grasped what happened at Calvary can seriously propose that law-keeping could have accomplished the same thing. The cross is either everything or it is nothing.

Those who seek justification by the law—moral or ceremonial—make void the grace of God in Christ. They may not intend to nullify the cross; but the logic of their position does exactly that. If righteousness is by the law, then Christ died without necessity. Paul will not accept that conclusion, and therefore he will not accept the premise that produces it.
